title: CREATE INDEX (non-concurrent, e.g. btree/gin/gist, incl. partial/expression)
lockMode: SHARE (ShareLock) on the indexed table, held for the ENTIRE build
sqlPattern: CREATE INDEX idx ON tbl (col);
safeWhen: Table is brand-new / not yet receiving production traffic (e.g. created in the same migration/transaction as the table).
Very small table where a single full scan completes in well under lock_timeout.
Dev/test/staging, or a maintenance window where a write outage on this table is acceptable.
You explicitly want the build inside a transaction block (non-concurrent is the ONLY form allowed in a txn block).
unsafeWhen: Table receives concurrent INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE in production — those writes block for the full duration of the build.
Large table: dwell time scales with row count (heap scan + sort + build), so the write block can last minutes to hours.
The SHARE lock request itself must queue behind an in-flight long-running read/txn; while it waits, ALL later statements (including plain SELECTs) queue behind it — the lock-queue stall.
safeRewrite: Build outside a transaction with CONCURRENTLY instead: `CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx ON tbl (col);` — takes SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (does not block reads or writes). Wrap operationally with a short `lock_timeout` and a retry loop, plus INVALID-index cleanup on failure.
edgeCases: SHARE conflicts with ROW EXCLUSIVE (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE), SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE, EXCLUSIVE, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — so it blocks writes and blocks/queues concurrent DDL and VACUUM, but permits ACCESS SHARE reads.
On a partitioned parent, CREATE INDEX recurses and takes SHARE on the parent and every partition, extending the write outage across all partitions.
Failure (e.g. disk/OOM/uniqueness) simply rolls back — non-concurrent builds do NOT leave an INVALID index behind (unlike CONCURRENTLY).
Reads that need the heap are fine, but the build competes for I/O and can be slowed by maintenance_work_mem limits.
versionNotes: Behavior stable across all supported majors (14–18); wording confirmed in current (18) docs.
maintenance_work_mem governs build memory/sort spill; not a correctness threshold.

title: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (CIC) — non-unique
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (ShareUpdateExclusiveLock) on the table
sqlPattern: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx ON tbl (col);
safeWhen: Production table under concurrent read/write load — CIC builds "without taking any locks that prevent concurrent inserts, updates, or deletes."
Run in its own session OUTSIDE any transaction block (autocommit).
No other concurrent index build is already running on the same table (only one at a time).
unsafeWhen: Issued inside a transaction block / multi-statement txn — CIC "cannot" run in a transaction block and will error (a plain CREATE INDEX can).
Target is a PARTITIONED parent table — concurrent builds on partitioned tables are NOT supported (still true through PG 18); it errors.
A second concurrent build is attempted on the same table simultaneously — "only one concurrent index build can occur on a table at a time."
Environment cannot tolerate the possibility of a leftover INVALID index that must be cleaned up out-of-band.
safeRewrite: This IS the safe rewrite for a plain CREATE INDEX. Harden it: SET lock_timeout to a small value (GoCardless/Xata suggest sub-2s) so the initial SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE acquisition can't stall the lock queue; retry with backoff; and on failure DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY the leftover invalid index before re-running. For partitioned tables: create index ON ONLY parent (invalid), CIC each partition, then ALTER INDEX ... ATTACH PARTITION.
edgeCases: Does TWO table scans and must WAIT for pre-existing transactions to finish before/around each scan and final validation — a single long-running transaction anywhere can stall completion indefinitely (though it does not block your writers).

Failed build (deadlock, cancel, lock_timeout, OOM, or uniqueness in the unique case) leaves an "invalid" index: "ignored for querying purposes because it might be incomplete; however it will still consume update overhead." Detect via `\d` showing INVALID, or `SELECT indisvalid FROM pg_index WHERE indexrelid='idx'::regclass` = false.

SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE conflicts with itself, SHARE, SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE, EXCLUSIVE, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — so it still blocks/queues concurrent DDL and VACUUM (and holds off/gets canceled by autovacuum) on that table.
Holds a snapshot for the duration, which can hold back VACUUM cleanup / cause bloat during very long builds.
Dependent views/queries won't use the index until it becomes valid; there is no partial-usability window.
versionNotes: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY available since PostgreSQL 8.2.
Concurrent index build on a PARTITIONED table: NOT supported in any release through 18 — must build per-partition and ATTACH.
Pre-PG 14, CIC held back the xmin horizon more aggressively (VACUUM couldn't remove dead tuples during any CIC on the DB); PG 14+ relaxed this so CIC/RIC no longer block VACUUM's xmin as broadly.

title: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX (non-concurrent)
lockMode: SHARE (ShareLock) on the table, held for the entire build
sqlPattern: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX idx ON tbl (col);
safeWhen: New/empty table, or small table, or maintenance window — same envelope as non-unique CREATE INDEX.
You are inside a transaction block and a brief write block is acceptable.
Existing data is known to satisfy the uniqueness constraint (no duplicates).
unsafeWhen: Production table under writes — blocks all writes for the whole scan/build.
Existing data contains duplicates — the build aborts with a uniqueness violation (wasting the whole locked scan).
Large table — write block dwell scales with rows.
safeRewrite: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (outside a txn), then — if a declared constraint is needed — ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT ... UNIQUE USING INDEX. De-duplicate data first to avoid a failed build.
edgeCases: Uniqueness is validated during the single locked scan; a duplicate aborts and rolls back cleanly (no leftover INVALID index in the non-concurrent form).
Same SHARE-lock lock-queue hazard as non-unique CREATE INDEX.
versionNotes: Stable across supported majors (14–18).

title: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (ShareUpdateExclusiveLock) on the table
sqlPattern: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx ON tbl (col);
safeWhen: Production, need reads+writes to continue during the build.
Existing data is already unique (pre-checked), and concurrent writers can tolerate the enforcement caveat below.
Run outside a transaction block.
unsafeWhen: Existing rows contain duplicates — the build fails AND leaves an INVALID unique index that KEEPS enforcing uniqueness.
Concurrent writers must not see spurious/early uniqueness errors — see enforcement caveat.
Inside a transaction block, or on a partitioned parent — errors (same restrictions as CIC).
safeRewrite: Pre-validate uniqueness (`SELECT col, count(*) FROM tbl GROUP BY col HAVING count(*)>1`), run CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY outside a txn with lock_timeout+retry, verify `pg_index.indisvalid`, and only then promote with ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT ... UNIQUE USING INDEX if a named constraint is required. On failure, DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY the invalid index before retrying.
edgeCases: CRITICAL: "the uniqueness constraint is already being enforced against other transactions when the second table scan begins" — so constraint violations "could be reported in other queries prior to the index becoming available for use, or even in cases where the index build eventually fails." Concurrent writers can get unique-violation errors from an index that is not yet usable.

CRITICAL: "if a failure does occur in the second scan, the 'invalid' index continues to enforce its uniqueness constraint afterwards." A leftover INVALID unique index is NOT inert — it can reject writes. It must be dropped (DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY) to restore normal write behavior.
All CIC caveats also apply: two scans, waits for transactions, one build per table, not in a txn block, not on partitioned parent.
versionNotes: Available since PostgreSQL 8.2 (unique + CONCURRENTLY).
Not supported on partitioned parent tables through PG 18.

title: Clean up a leftover INVALID index after a failed CONCURRENTLY build (DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY)
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (ShareUpdateExclusiveLock) on the table
sqlPattern: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx;  -- after confirming pg_index.indisvalid = false
safeWhen: Removing a standalone INVALID index left behind by a failed CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (including an invalid UNIQUE index that is now wrongly enforcing uniqueness).
The index does NOT back a UNIQUE or PRIMARY KEY constraint.
Run outside a transaction block, one index at a time.
unsafeWhen: The index supports a UNIQUE or PRIMARY KEY constraint — "an index that supports a UNIQUE or PRIMARY KEY constraint cannot be dropped this way"; use ALTER TABLE ... DROP CONSTRAINT instead (which takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE).
Inside a transaction block — DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY "cannot" run in a txn block.
You need CASCADE (dependent objects) — not supported by CONCURRENTLY.
Index is on a partitioned table — cannot be dropped with this option.
safeRewrite: Detect first: `SELECT indexrelid::regclass, indisvalid FROM pg_index WHERE NOT indisvalid;`. Then `DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY <name>;` outside a txn. If it backs a constraint, use `ALTER TABLE ... DROP CONSTRAINT ...` in a lock_timeout+retry wrapper (ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, but metadata-only/brief).
edgeCases: Recommended PG recovery for a failed CIC: "drop the index and try again to perform CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY" (or REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY).
DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY still waits for conflicting transactions to complete before finishing, so a long-running txn can delay (not block) the drop.
Only ONE index name per command; no CASCADE.
strong_migrations can auto-remove these on rerun via StrongMigrations.remove_invalid_indexes = true.
versionNotes: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY available since PostgreSQL 9.2.
Restriction that it cannot drop constraint-backing indexes or partitioned-table indexes holds through PG 18.

title: DROP INDEX (non-concurrent) — including dropping an invalid index the blocking way
sqlPattern: DROP INDEX idx;
safeWhen: Low-traffic table or maintenance window where a brief total lock is acceptable.
You need CASCADE, or must drop multiple indexes atomically, or must run inside a transaction block.
Dropping an index that backs a constraint is only doable this way (or via ALTER TABLE DROP CONSTRAINT).
unsafeWhen: Busy production table — "a normal DROP INDEX acquires an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the table, blocking other accesses until the index drop can be completed."

A long-running transaction is holding any lock on the table — the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request queues and then blocks ALL reads and writes behind it (lock-queue stall), even though the drop itself is metadata-only/fast.
safeRewrite: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx; (outside a txn, single index, no CASCADE, not for constraint-backing indexes). If ACCESS EXCLUSIVE is unavoidable (constraint index / CASCADE), guard with a small lock_timeout and retry to avoid poisoning the lock queue.
edgeCases: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE conflicts with locks of ALL modes — total exclusion of reads and writes for the (usually brief) duration.
The dwell is normally constant (catalog delete + file unlink), so the danger is the lock QUEUE, not the work itself.
Dropping an index referenced by a foreign-key enforcement path can slow FK checks afterward; verify no query depends on it.
versionNotes: Behavior stable across supported majors (14–18).

title: REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY — rebuild an INVALID or bloated index in place
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (ShareUpdateExclusiveLock) on the table
sqlPattern: REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx;
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12+, rebuilding a valid-but-bloated index, or recovering an INVALID index left by a failed CIC without dropping/recreating it.
Production under load — does not block reads or writes.
Run outside a transaction block.
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL < 12 — only plain REINDEX exists there, which takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and blocks reads and writes.
Inside a transaction block — the CONCURRENTLY form cannot run in a txn block.
You cannot tolerate a possible leftover transient index (_ccnew/_ccold) if the rebuild fails.
safeRewrite: On PG12+, `REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx;` is itself the online rewrite for a blocking `REINDEX`. On PG<12, drop+recreate with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY instead of plain REINDEX. Wrap with lock_timeout+retry.
edgeCases: PG docs list REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY as an alternative recovery path for a failed CIC ("rebuild the index with REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY").
A failed/interrupted REINDEX CONCURRENTLY can itself leave a transient INVALID index named with a `_ccnew` or `_ccold` suffix that must be dropped (DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY).
Plain (non-concurrent) REINDEX and REINDEX TABLE take ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — do not confuse them with the CONCURRENTLY variant.
REINDEX CONCURRENTLY of an index backing a constraint keeps the constraint intact (unlike DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY, which refuses constraint indexes).
versionNotes: REINDEX INDEX/TABLE CONCURRENTLY introduced in PostgreSQL 12. Not available in 11 or earlier.
Same partitioned-table caveats apply; REINDEX on a partitioned index processes partitions.

title: Promote a concurrently-built UNIQUE index to a named UNIQUE/PRIMARY KEY constraint (ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT ... USING INDEX)
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (AccessExclusiveLock) on the table, briefly
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT uq UNIQUE USING INDEX idx;  -- idx built earlier with CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY
safeWhen: The supporting unique index already exists, is VALID, and was built with CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (so the expensive scan/validation already happened online).
You need a declared UNIQUE or PRIMARY KEY constraint (e.g. as an FK target) rather than a bare unique index.
Guarded by a small lock_timeout + retry so the brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE cannot poison the lock queue.
unsafeWhen: The index is INVALID or not unique — ALTER TABLE ... USING INDEX requires a valid unique index; it will reject an invalid one.
For PRIMARY KEY: any column is nullable — PK promotion sets columns NOT NULL, and on older majors that implied a full table scan/rewrite risk; ensure columns are already NOT NULL.
Run without lock_timeout on a busy table — the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE acquisition can queue behind a long transaction and stall all traffic.
safeRewrite: Two-step online pattern (Braintree/strong_migrations): (1) CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx ...; verify indisvalid. (2) ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT uq UNIQUE USING INDEX idx; inside a lock_timeout+retry wrapper. This moves all scanning off the exclusive lock; the ALTER itself is catalog-only.
edgeCases: USING INDEX reuses the existing index — no re-scan, no re-validation — so cost is metadata-only; the ONLY hazard is the brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and its lock-queue.
The index name is consumed/renamed to the constraint's naming; the constraint and index become linked (dropping the constraint drops the index).
Cannot combine with other subcommands that would force a rewrite in the same ALTER TABLE if you want to keep it metadata-only.
Dependent views/FKs referencing the columns are unaffected; adding a PK enables it as an FK target.
versionNotes: ADD CONSTRAINT ... UNIQUE/PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX available since PostgreSQL 9.1.
Metadata-only ALTER TABLE optimizations (e.g. for other subcommands) vary by version, but USING INDEX promotion has always been catalog-only for the constraint itself.

title: ADD COLUMN, nullable, no DEFAULT
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c <type>;
safeRewrite: Already the safe form. Still wrap it: SET lock_timeout = '2s' (and statement_timeout) before the DDL, and retry on 55P03 lock_not_available so a busy table cannot let the pending ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request pile up the lock queue. Add the column in a deploy BEFORE any app code references it.
blocksReadsNote: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE conflicts with ALL lock modes, so it momentarily blocks even a plain SELECT; for a catalog-only ADD COLUMN the hold is sub-millisecond once the lock is granted.
safeWhen: Any supported PostgreSQL version (this is catalog-only on all versions)
The ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock is acquired quickly (short lock_timeout + retry)
unsafeWhen: A long-running transaction or query holds a conflicting lock: the ALTER's ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request goes into the lock queue and every subsequent statement (including ACCESS SHARE SELECTs) queues BEHIND it, so reads and writes stall for the duration of the blocker
Run with default lock_timeout=0 (wait forever) on a hot table
edgeCases: The block is momentary UNLESS the lock cannot be granted immediately — then the lock-queue pile-up is the real outage, not the DDL itself (GoCardless/Xata)
Dependent views/rules are unaffected by adding a column
versionNotes: Metadata-only on all supported versions (7.x onward)

title: ADD COLUMN with constant / non-volatile DEFAULT (nullable)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int DEFAULT 0;   -- also now(), CURRENT_TIMESTAMP (STABLE)
safeRewrite: On PG >= 11: safe as written (fast-default); still guard with a short lock_timeout + retry. On PG <= 10: split it — (1) ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int; (2) ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c SET DEFAULT 0; (3) backfill existing rows in batches; this avoids the full rewrite.
safeWhen: PG >= 11 AND the DEFAULT expression is non-volatile (a literal constant, or a STABLE/IMMUTABLE function such as now()/CURRENT_TIMESTAMP which is evaluated ONCE at statement time and stored)
Lock acquired quickly
unsafeWhen: PG <= 10: ANY non-null DEFAULT forces a full table + index REWRITE under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (dwell scales with table+TOAST bytes)
The default expression is VOLATILE (see the volatile-default entry) — version does not matter, it always rewrites
edgeCases: Fast-default stores the evaluated value in pg_attribute.attmissingval with atthasmissing=true; existing rows synthesize it on read with no on-disk change
atthasmissing/attmissingval are CLEARED whenever the table is later rewritten (VACUUM FULL, CLUSTER, another rewriting ALTER) — harmless, just materializes the value
CAUTION on the volatility check: now()/CURRENT_TIMESTAMP are STABLE (fast path, single value); clock_timestamp() is VOLATILE (rewrite). Do not conflate them.
versionNotes: PG 11 (2018) introduced the fast-default optimization: ~1400x speedup (e.g. 27s -> 19ms on 10M rows in depesz's test)
PG <= 10: constant default = full rewrite

title: ADD COLUMN NOT NULL with constant / non-volatile DEFAULT
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
safeRewrite: On PG >= 11: safe as written (fast-default satisfies NOT NULL because every existing row reads the stored attmissingval). On PG <= 10: (1) ADD COLUMN c int; (2) SET DEFAULT 0; (3) backfill in batches; (4) add CHECK (c IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID, VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (scans without ACCESS EXCLUSIVE), then SET NOT NULL — on PG >= 12 SET NOT NULL can reuse the validated CHECK and skip a second scan.
safeWhen: PG >= 11 AND non-volatile DEFAULT is present (NOT NULL is enforced for existing rows via the missing value, no scan needed)
unsafeWhen: PG <= 10: full REWRITE
NOT NULL WITHOUT a default on a non-empty table: fails immediately — existing rows would violate NOT NULL
Volatile default: rewrites regardless of version
edgeCases: Because the missing value is non-null, the NOT NULL is trivially satisfied for pre-existing rows on PG >= 11 with no validation scan
This is the historically dangerous 'ADD COLUMN ... NOT NULL DEFAULT' that Braintree/GoCardless warned about — the warning is only accurate for PG <= 10 (or volatile defaults)
versionNotes: Safe/fast since PG 11
PG 12+: SET NOT NULL can use an existing valid CHECK constraint to avoid a full-table scan

title: ADD COLUMN with VOLATILE DEFAULT
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN id uuid DEFAULT gen_random_uuid();   -- clock_timestamp(), random(), uuid_generate_v4()
safeRewrite: (1) ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN id uuid;  (2) ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN id SET DEFAULT gen_random_uuid();  (only NEW rows get it) (3) backfill existing rows in batches (UPDATE ... WHERE id IS NULL LIMIT N, committing each batch, outside one big txn); (4) if required, add NOT NULL via NOT VALID CHECK + VALIDATE.
safeWhen: Never safe as a single statement on a non-empty table — always rewrites
unsafeWhen: ALL versions: a volatile DEFAULT must be evaluated per-row, so the fast-default path cannot apply and the entire table + all indexes are rewritten while holding ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (dwell scales with heap+TOAST bytes)
edgeCases: gen_random_uuid(), uuid_generate_v4(), random(), clock_timestamp(), nextval() are VOLATILE
Rewrite needs up to ~2x the table's disk space transiently and rewrites every index; TOASTed values are re-written too
The docs list volatile DEFAULT explicitly among rewrite-causing forms
versionNotes: Rewrites on all versions, including PG 11+ (fast-default requires non-volatile default)

title: ADD COLUMN GENERATED ALWAYS AS (...) STORED
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int GENERATED ALWAYS AS (a + b) STORED;
safeRewrite: Add a plain nullable column and populate it with a BEFORE INSERT/UPDATE trigger (or app logic) plus a batched backfill; on PG >= 18 a VIRTUAL generated column is a rewrite-free alternative for read-time computation.
safeWhen: Never safe as a single statement on a non-empty table
unsafeWhen: ALL versions (PG 12+, where the feature exists): a STORED generated column must be materialized for every row, forcing a full table + index REWRITE under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
edgeCases: Stored generated columns are PG 12+ only
You cannot 'SET DEFAULT then backfill' — generated values are not settable; you must switch to a plain column + trigger
Full-heap rewrite: same TOAST/disk-doubling/index-rewrite costs as any rewrite
versionNotes: STORED generated columns added in PG 12; always rewrite

title: ADD COLUMN GENERATED ALWAYS AS (...) [VIRTUAL]
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int GENERATED ALWAYS AS (a + b) VIRTUAL;
safeRewrite: Safe as written on PG >= 18; still guard with short lock_timeout + retry. VIRTUAL is the default when neither STORED nor VIRTUAL is specified on PG 18+.
safeWhen: PG >= 18: virtual generated columns are computed on read and require NO rewrite
unsafeWhen: PG <= 17: VIRTUAL is not supported (only STORED exists) — the statement errors, not a safety issue but a compatibility one
edgeCases: The docs state explicitly: 'Adding a virtual generated column never requires a rewrite.'
Cannot be indexed the same way as a stored column in early support; verify per-version constraints
Still takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE momentarily like any ADD COLUMN
versionNotes: Virtual generated columns introduced in PG 18; became the default generated-column kind

title: ADD COLUMN GENERATED {ALWAYS|BY DEFAULT} AS IDENTITY
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN id bigint GENERATED ALWAYS AS IDENTITY;
safeRewrite: (1) CREATE SEQUENCE t_id_seq;  (2) ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN id bigint;  (3) ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN id SET DEFAULT nextval('t_id_seq') (new rows only);  (4) backfill existing rows in batches;  (5) optionally SET NOT NULL via NOT VALID CHECK + VALIDATE, and (PG 10.2+) attach ownership. Note this yields a serial-like column, not a true identity column.
safeWhen: Never safe as a single statement on a non-empty table
unsafeWhen: ALL versions (PG 10+): the docs explicitly list 'an identity column' among the forms that rewrite the entire table and its indexes (every existing row must receive a distinct sequence value)
edgeCases: Identity columns are PG 10+
Identity implies NOT NULL and a per-row sequence draw (volatile-like), hence the rewrite
On an empty table this is instantaneous — the rewrite cost is zero rows
versionNotes: Identity columns added in PG 10; always rewrite when the table has rows

title: ADD COLUMN of a DOMAIN type
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c my_domain;
safeRewrite: If the domain carries constraints, treat like a scan/validate: add the underlying base-type column (fast-default rules apply), backfill, then migrate to the domain-typed column or add the check separately. If the domain has no constraints, it behaves exactly like its base type.
safeWhen: The domain has NO constraints (no CHECK, no NOT NULL): behaves like the base type, so PG >= 11 constant-default rules give metadata-only
unsafeWhen: The domain HAS constraints: the docs state 'a column with a domain data type that has constraints will cause the entire table and its indexes to be rewritten' (every row is validated against the domain constraint)
edgeCases: The rewrite trigger is the presence of domain constraints, not the domain itself — inspect pg_constraint / pg_type for the domain
Combines with default rules: a constrained domain rewrites even with a constant default
versionNotes: Behavior consistent across supported versions; interacts with PG 11 fast-default only when the domain is unconstrained

title: ADD COLUMN ... UNIQUE (inline unique constraint)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int UNIQUE;
safeRewrite: (1) ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int;  (2) backfill so values are distinct;  (3) CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY t_c_key ON t (c);  (4) ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT t_c_key UNIQUE USING INDEX t_c_key. Steps 3-4 avoid the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE full-scan build.
safeWhen: Table is empty (index build scans zero rows)
unsafeWhen: Non-empty table: the inline UNIQUE builds a unique index while holding ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, scanning every existing row (dwell scales with rows); you cannot use CONCURRENTLY inside ADD COLUMN
Column is NOT NULL with a constant default: all rows share the default value -> immediate unique violation, statement fails
edgeCases: Nullable + no default: all rows are NULL and NULLs are never equal, so the constraint is satisfiable, but the index is still built under lock
A failed non-concurrent build rolls back cleanly; a later CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY that fails leaves an INVALID index that must be DROPped before retry
versionNotes: USING INDEX to attach a concurrently-built unique index: PG 9.1+

title: ADD COLUMN ... PRIMARY KEY (inline)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN id bigint PRIMARY KEY;
safeRewrite: For a populated table you cannot add a usable PK column in one statement. Instead: add the column, populate distinct non-null values (e.g. via a sequence backfill), CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, then ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT ... PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX; SET NOT NULL is implied by the PK.
safeWhen: Table is empty
unsafeWhen: Non-empty table: builds a unique index under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (SCAN) AND enforces NOT NULL

PK requires NOT NULL: a brand-new column is all NULL, so with no default the statement fails on the NOT NULL; with a constant default all rows are identical, so it fails on uniqueness — inline PRIMARY KEY on a populated table is effectively unusable in one shot
edgeCases: Combines the NOT-NULL-violation and unique-violation traps — almost always must be decomposed
USING INDEX path lets you attach a CONCURRENTLY-built unique index as the PK to avoid the lock-held scan
versionNotes: ADD CONSTRAINT ... PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX available PG 9.1+

title: ADD COLUMN ... REFERENCES other(id) (inline foreign key)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN parent_id bigint REFERENCES other(id);
safeRewrite: (1) ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN parent_id bigint;  (2) ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT fk FOREIGN KEY (parent_id) REFERENCES other(id) NOT VALID;  (3) ALTER TABLE t VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk (takes only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, does not block reads/writes). This keeps the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on 'other' as brief as possible.
safeWhen: The new column is all NULL (default), so FK validation of existing rows is trivial (NULLs are exempt from FK checks) — no scan of t is needed
unsafeWhen: The inline form takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on t AND a lock on the REFERENCED table 'other' (SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE +) at the same time — two-table lock acquisition raises deadlock/queue risk on hot referenced tables
If a constant default pointing at a real key is used, existing rows are validated against 'other' (a scan)
edgeCases: GoCardless's canonical example: adding an FK locks both referencing and referenced tables at ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — split creation (NOT VALID) and validation to shrink the lock window
VALIDATE CONSTRAINT uses SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, allowing concurrent reads and writes
versionNotes: ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT VALID for FKs: PG 9.1+; the referenced-table lock was reduced from ACCESS EXCLUSIVE to SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE in PG 9.5
_correction: ADD FOREIGN KEY takes only SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE on the referenced table — NOT an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on its PK index (removed). VALIDATE CONSTRAINT of an FK also takes ROW SHARE on the referenced table (non-blocking).

title: ADD COLUMN ... CHECK (...) (inline check constraint)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int CHECK (c > 0);
safeRewrite: Add the column first, then add the CHECK separately as NOT VALID and VALIDATE it: (1) ADD COLUMN c int; (2) backfill; (3) ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT c_chk CHECK (c > 0) NOT VALID; (4) VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, non-blocking).
safeWhen: New column is NULL / has a constant default that satisfies the predicate: the check on existing rows is evaluated against the (single) missing value, which is cheap and needs no per-row heap scan on PG >= 11 fast-default
unsafeWhen: If adding the column also forces a rewrite (volatile default, pre-11 default, etc.), the CHECK is validated during that rewrite under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
Inline CHECK cannot be declared NOT VALID — you lose the ability to defer validation; use a separate ADD CONSTRAINT to get NOT VALID
edgeCases: Inline constraints are always created VALID, so you cannot split validation off the lock-holding statement — decompose to get NOT VALID + VALIDATE
A CHECK that references only the new column with a constant default is satisfiable for all existing rows via the missing value
versionNotes: CHECK ... NOT VALID + VALIDATE CONSTRAINT: PG 9.2+

title: Increase varchar(n) length or drop the length limit (varchar(n) -> varchar(m>=n) or varchar)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c TYPE varchar(500); -- from varchar(200)
safeWhen: New max length >= old max length
Removing the length limit entirely (varchar(n) -> varchar / text)
No collation change on the column
PostgreSQL 9.2+
unsafeWhen: Server is older than 9.2 (any typmod change rewrote the table)
A concurrent long-running query holds AccessShare on the table, forcing the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request to queue and stall all following queries
safeRewrite: Run directly: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c TYPE varchar(m) (m>=n). No heap rewrite and no index rebuild occur. Wrap in a bounded lock acquisition to avoid the lock-queue stampede: SET lock_timeout = '2s'; ALTER TABLE ...; and retry on failure. varchar_transform strips the length-coercion node so it is a pure catalog change.
edgeCases: Even though dwell is constant, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE conflicts with every lock mode, so a blocked ALTER sits at the head of the lock queue and blocks all subsequent SELECTs/INSERTs until it acquires (GoCardless lock-queue problem)
Indexes are NOT rebuilt because varchar sort order is unchanged and there is no collation change
Prefer plain text or varchar with a CHECK constraint over varchar(n) so future length changes are constraint edits, not type changes
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 9.2 (Noah Misch): 'Increasing the length limit for a varchar or varbit column, or removing the limit altogether, no longer requires a table rewrite.'

title: Decrease varchar(n) length (varchar(n) -> varchar(m<n))
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c TYPE varchar(50); -- from varchar(200)
safeWhen: Never rewrite-free: reducing the limit always applies a length-checking coercion to every row
unsafeWhen: Always unsafe on a large/high-traffic table: full heap + index rewrite under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, dwell scales with table bytes
Any existing value longer than m aborts the whole statement with error 22001 (value too long)
safeRewrite: Do NOT change the type. To enforce a shorter length without a rewrite, add a validated CHECK constraint in two steps: (1) ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT c_len CHECK (length(c) <= 50) NOT VALID; (2) ALTER TABLE t VALIDATE CONSTRAINT c_len; — step 2 takes only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE and scans without blocking writes. If the column type must truly shrink, use the add-column/backfill/dual-write/swap dance (see int-to-bigint) or pgroll/logical replication.
edgeCases: Postgres does not truncate; it errors on the first over-length row, so the migration can fail mid-flight
varchar_transform returns NULL when the new typmod is smaller, forcing ATRewriteTable (a genuine heap rewrite, not just a verification scan)
Rewrite also rebuilds all indexes and re-TOASTs large values
versionNotes: No version avoids this rewrite; the 9.2 optimization only covers length increases/removals

title: varchar / varchar(n) -> text
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c TYPE text;
safeWhen: Always safe: text is binary coercible from varchar and they sort identically
No collation change
PostgreSQL 9.2+ (for the varchar(n) case; unconstrained varchar->text was always cheap)
unsafeWhen: Older than 9.2 when dropping a length limit as part of the change
Lock-queue contention as with any ACCESS EXCLUSIVE statement
safeRewrite: Run directly under a lock_timeout guard: SET lock_timeout='2s'; ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c TYPE text;. No heap rewrite, and indexes are explicitly NOT rebuilt.
edgeCases: PG docs: 'in the absence of a collation change, a column can be changed from text to varchar (or vice versa) without rebuilding the indexes because these data types sort identically'
If the column participates in a view/rule, the ALTER still succeeds because the type is coercible, but expression indexes referencing varchar-specific behavior are the exception to watch
versionNotes: Binary-coercibility no-rewrite rule is documented in ALTER TABLE Notes; length-limit removal made rewrite-free in 9.2

title: text -> varchar (no length limit)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c TYPE varchar;
safeWhen: Target is unconstrained varchar (no (n))
No collation change
Binary coercible both directions
unsafeWhen: Target has a length limit — that is the separate text-to-varchar(n) case and IS a rewrite
Lock-queue contention
safeRewrite: Run directly under lock_timeout. No rewrite, no index rebuild.
edgeCases: strong_migrations flags text->string as safe only 'with no :limit'; adding any (n) turns it into a coercion/rewrite
Same identical-sort-order index exemption as varchar<->text
versionNotes: Rewrite-free per the binary-coercibility exception in ALTER TABLE Notes

title: text (or varchar) -> varchar(n) with a length limit
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c TYPE varchar(100); -- from text
safeWhen: Only rewrite-free if the source is already varchar(m) with m<=n (i.e. a length increase); from text it is never rewrite-free
unsafeWhen: Source is text (unbounded): a length-checking coercion runs against every row -> full heap+index rewrite
Any value longer than n aborts with error 22001
safeRewrite: Prefer keeping the column as text and enforcing length with a CHECK added NOT VALID then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, no rewrite, no write block). If a true varchar(n) type is mandatory, use add-column/backfill/dual-write/swap or pgroll.
edgeCases: This is the asymmetry trap: varchar->text is free but text->varchar(n) is a rewrite; a tool must inspect the target typmod, not just the type name
TOASTed long values are fully de-TOASTed and re-stored during the rewrite
versionNotes: No version removes this rewrite; 9.2 only helps when the change is a length increase within varchar

title: integer -> bigint (also smallint->int, int->numeric: any change in on-disk width)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN id TYPE bigint;
safeWhen: Never rewrite-free: int4 (4 bytes) and int8 (8 bytes) have different on-disk representations and are not binary coercible
unsafeWhen: Always unsafe at scale: full heap + all-index rewrite under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE; dwell scales with table bytes and can run for many minutes to hours, blocking reads and writes the entire time
safeRewrite: Zero-downtime pattern: (1) ADD COLUMN new_id bigint (NULLable, non-volatile default -> metadata-only in PG 11+); (2) dual-write via BEFORE INSERT/UPDATE trigger setting new_id := id; (3) backfill in bounded batches (UPDATE ... WHERE id BETWEEN ... with commits) to avoid long transactions and bloat; (4) CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on new_id, add matching constraints; (5) in one short ACCESS EXCLUSIVE txn swap: drop old, rename new_id->id, repoint FKs/sequence. Alternatively use logical replication to a rebuilt table or pgroll for an expand/contract migration.
edgeCases: If id is a PRIMARY KEY the sequence must be switched to bigint and every referencing FK column must also become bigint (multi-step, coordinated)
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY that fails or is interrupted leaves an INVALID index that still consumes writes/space and must be DROPPED and rebuilt
Backfilling in one statement bloats the table and holds row locks; batch it
Watch for the 2.1B int4 overflow deadline that motivates the change — plan before the sequence approaches max
versionNotes: No version makes int->bigint rewrite-free. ADD COLUMN with a non-volatile DEFAULT became metadata-only in PostgreSQL 11, which is what makes step 1 cheap.

title: numeric/decimal precision increase at same scale, or constrained numeric -> unconstrained numeric
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN amt TYPE numeric(12,2); -- from numeric(8,2)
safeWhen: New precision >= old precision AND scale is unchanged
Changing from numeric(p,s) to unconstrained numeric
PostgreSQL 9.2+
unsafeWhen: Scale changes (up OR down) -> rewrite
Precision decreases -> rewrite (and may error on over-precision values)
Server older than 9.2
safeRewrite: Run directly under lock_timeout when only precision grows at fixed scale, or when dropping the constraint to plain numeric. numeric_transform elides the coercion so it is catalog-only.
edgeCases: Increasing scale is NOT safe even though it feels non-lossy — it changes the stored representation and forces a rewrite
Decreasing precision/scale rounds or rejects values via a full rewrite
Unconstrained numeric is the most future-proof choice if the precision is not a hard business rule
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 9.2 (Noah Misch): 'increasing the allowable precision of a numeric column, or changing a column from constrained numeric to unconstrained numeric, no longer requires a table rewrite.'

title: numeric precision decrease, or any scale change
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN amt TYPE numeric(8,4); -- scale 2 -> 4, or precision shrink
safeWhen: Never rewrite-free
unsafeWhen: Any scale change (numeric_transform cannot elide the coercion)
Any precision decrease
Over-precision existing values cause a numeric field overflow error aborting the statement
safeRewrite: Enforce tighter numeric bounds with a CHECK constraint (ADD ... NOT VALID then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT) instead of retyping, or use add-column/backfill/swap if the scale genuinely must change.
edgeCases: Rewrite rebuilds all indexes and re-TOASTs
Rounding on scale reduction silently changes values — a correctness as well as a locking concern
versionNotes: The 9.2 optimization deliberately excludes scale changes and precision reductions

title: timestamp (without time zone) -> timestamptz
sqlPattern: SET TimeZone='UTC'; ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN ts TYPE timestamptz;
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12+ AND the session TimeZone is exactly 'UTC' -> METADATA_ONLY (binary compatible, no rewrite, no index rebuild)
unsafeWhen: Session TimeZone is anything other than UTC -> REWRITE (dwell scales with bytes)
PostgreSQL 11 or older -> always REWRITE regardless of time zone
safeRewrite: On PG 12+: explicitly SET TimeZone='UTC' in the same session, guard with lock_timeout, then ALTER ... TYPE timestamptz. Verify server default with SHOW timezone first. If not on UTC/PG12+, use add-column/backfill/swap with an explicit USING (ts AT TIME ZONE 'UTC') so the intended source zone is unambiguous.
edgeCases: The UTC fast path is a pure reinterpretation: it does NOT convert values, it treats stored wall-clock times as UTC. If your stored timestamps were really in another zone, the fast path bakes in wrong instants — a silent data-correctness bug
In the non-UTC rewrite, values are interpreted using the session TimeZone via the implicit cast; set it deliberately
Dependent views/rules referencing the column can block the ALTER with 'cannot alter type of a column used by a view or rule'
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 12: 'ALTER TABLE ... SET DATA TYPE can now change between timestamp and timestamptz to avoid a table rewrite when the session time zone is UTC.' Groundwork for interval/timestamp began in 9.2 but the UTC no-rewrite rule for timestamp<->timestamptz is 12.

title: timestamptz -> timestamp (without time zone)
sqlPattern: SET TimeZone='UTC'; ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN ts TYPE timestamp;
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12+ AND session TimeZone = 'UTC' -> METADATA_ONLY
unsafeWhen: Non-UTC session -> REWRITE
PG 11 or older -> always REWRITE
safeRewrite: Same as timestamp->timestamptz: on PG12+ pin TimeZone='UTC' and guard with lock_timeout; otherwise add-column/backfill/swap with an explicit USING clause naming the zone.
edgeCases: Dropping the time zone in a non-UTC session converts each value to local wall-clock and discards the offset — lossy and usually a mistake
Generally discouraged: prefer timestamptz everywhere (PG wiki 'Don't Do This')
versionNotes: Same PostgreSQL 12 UTC-binary-compatibility rule applies symmetrically

title: Add a value to an enum type (ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE)
sqlPattern: ALTER TYPE mood ADD VALUE IF NOT EXISTS 'ecstatic' AFTER 'happy';
lockMode: AccessExclusiveLock on the enum type row in pg_type (updates pg_enum); does NOT lock or rewrite tables that use the enum
safeWhen: Adding a new value is always cheap and does not touch table data
Appending at the end is cheapest
PostgreSQL 12+ if you need it inside a transaction block (with the same-txn usage restriction)
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL 11 or older AND run inside a transaction block that did not create the enum -> ERROR (cannot run ADD VALUE in a transaction block)
Using the newly added value in the SAME transaction that added it -> ERROR until commit (all versions when inside a txn block)
safeRewrite: Run ADD VALUE ... IF NOT EXISTS directly (outside a transaction block on <=11; standalone statement on 12+). Roll out via expand/contract: (1) add the value, (2) deploy app code that can read/handle it and write it, (3) backfill. To REMOVE or reorder values (not supported natively), migrate the column to text/a lookup table, or create a new enum, swap the column type, and drop the old enum.
edgeCases: There is no ALTER TYPE ... DROP VALUE — additions are effectively irreversible without recreating the type
Values added with BEFORE/AFTER (non-tail) can make comparisons slightly slower; also happens at tail if the OID counter wrapped since type creation
Committing the ADD VALUE is required before any query, index, or CHECK can reference the new label (the 'cannot use in same transaction' rule)
For rapidly-evolving value sets, a foreign-key lookup table is more migration-friendly than an enum
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 12: 'Previously, ALTER TYPE ... ADD VALUE could not be called in a transaction block, unless it was part of the same transaction that created the enumerated type. Now it can be called in a later transaction, so long as the new enumerated value is not referenced until after it is committed.'

title: ALTER COLUMN ... SET NOT NULL (naive, no supporting constraint)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN col SET NOT NULL;
safeRewrite: Do NOT run bare on a large/hot table. Use the fast-path sequence: (1) ADD CONSTRAINT c CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID; (2) VALIDATE CONSTRAINT c; (3) SET NOT NULL (skips the scan on PG 12+); (4) optionally DROP CONSTRAINT c. On PG 18+ use the native NOT NULL ... NOT VALID + VALIDATE CONSTRAINT sequence instead.
safeWhen: Table is small enough that a full scan under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE finishes within your lock_timeout budget (roughly a few thousand rows / sub-100ms)
You are inside a maintenance window with no concurrent traffic
The table was just created in the same transaction / not yet visible to other sessions
unsafeWhen: Table is large (scan dwell scales with row count)

There is concurrent read/write traffic — the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock blocks SELECTs and DML for the entire scan, and any query already holding an ACCESS SHARE lock delays lock acquisition while all newer queries queue behind the pending ALTER
edgeCases: Even a 'fast' ALTER can cause an outage via the lock queue: while the ALTER waits for ACCESS EXCLUSIVE behind one slow query, every subsequent SELECT/INSERT queues behind the ALTER
On PG 18, if the column already has an INVALID not-null constraint, bare SET NOT NULL will VALIDATE it (still a full scan under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE)
versionNotes: All versions: bare SET NOT NULL scans the whole table unless a valid proving CHECK (PG 12+) or a valid not-null constraint (PG 18+) already exists

title: Step 1 — ADD CONSTRAINT CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT t_col_not_null CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID;
safeRewrite: Run as-is, but wrap with a short lock_timeout (e.g. SET lock_timeout = '2s') plus retry-with-backoff so it fails fast instead of queueing traffic. NOT VALID is essential — it skips the verifying scan, so the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock is held only for the catalog write.
safeWhen: NOT VALID is specified (skips the table scan; only a catalog row is written)
Guarded by a short lock_timeout and retry loop
unsafeWhen: You omit NOT VALID — the ADD CONSTRAINT then scans the whole table under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (becomes SCAN, blocks reads+writes for the full scan)
No lock_timeout: acquiring ACCESS EXCLUSIVE can stall behind a long-running txn and pile all incoming queries behind it
edgeCases: The constraint immediately begins enforcing on new INSERT/UPDATE even while NOT VALID — a row with NULL col inserted after this step will fail, which is the intended forward-fill behavior

If validation later fails or is interrupted, a leftover NOT VALID constraint remains and keeps enforcing on writes; make the step idempotent (IF NOT EXISTS / catch duplicate_object) and clean up on abort
Constraint name collisions across migrations — pick a deterministic, unique name
versionNotes: NOT VALID for CHECK constraints available since PG 9.2
Docs: 'if a valid CHECK constraint exists (and is not dropped in the same command) which proves no NULL can exist, then the table scan is skipped' — this is what makes step 1 worthwhile

title: Step 2 — VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (the online scan)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t VALIDATE CONSTRAINT t_col_not_null;
safeRewrite: Run as-is. This is the online scan: it reads every row but takes only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, which does NOT conflict with ACCESS SHARE (SELECT) or ROW EXCLUSIVE (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE). No lock_timeout race is needed for DML, though it does serialize against other DDL and blocks/BLOCKED-by VACUUM.
safeWhen: Run as a separate statement/transaction after the NOT VALID constraint is committed
Concurrent reads and writes must continue — this step is designed to be online
unsafeWhen: Never rewrite this back into a single transaction with the ADD CONSTRAINT (without NOT VALID) — that collapses to an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE scan
Running while a conflicting DDL or a non-CONCURRENT VACUUM/ANALYZE holds SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE will make them wait on each other
edgeCases: Docs: 'validation acquires only a SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock' because concurrent writers already enforce the constraint, so only pre-existing rows are checked
SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE self-conflicts: it blocks and is blocked by autovacuum on the same table, CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, ANALYZE, and other ALTERs — long validations can defer autovacuum
If any existing row violates the predicate, VALIDATE errors out and the constraint stays NOT VALID (no partial state); fix data and re-run
versionNotes: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock for VALIDATE CONSTRAINT documented since PG 9.4+ and unchanged through PG 18

title: Step 3 — SET NOT NULL with a VALIDATED proving CHECK present (PG 12+ fast path)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN col SET NOT NULL;  -- valid CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) already present
safeRewrite: Run as-is on PG 12+ with the validated CHECK still in place, guarded by a short lock_timeout + retry. Cost is METADATA_ONLY *only when* a valid CHECK (or valid not-null constraint on PG 18+) that proves col IS NOT NULL exists and is not dropped in the same statement; otherwise it degrades to a full SCAN under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12 or newer
A VALID CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) constraint (or valid not-null constraint on PG 18+) exists on that exact column and is not dropped in the same command
Guarded by lock_timeout + retry so the brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE acquisition cannot pile up traffic
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL 11 or older — no scan-skip optimization exists, so SET NOT NULL always scans (this variant does not apply; treat as set-not-null-naive)
The proving CHECK is still NOT VALID — SET NOT NULL cannot trust it and falls back to a full scan
You DROP the CHECK in the same ALTER statement as SET NOT NULL — the scan is then NOT skipped
The CHECK predicate doesn't literally prove non-nullness of that column (e.g. CHECK (col > 0) does prove it, but a partial/OR'd predicate may not)
edgeCases: Docs (PG 12+): 'unless ... a valid CHECK constraint exists (and is not dropped in the same command) which proves no NULL can exist, then the table scan is skipped'
Even in METADATA_ONLY mode the lock is ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and blocks reads+writes for its (short) duration — still subject to the lock-queue outage pattern
On partitioned tables SET NOT NULL on the parent recurses to all partitions; each needs a proving constraint to skip its own scan

A CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) is not semantically identical to attnotnull for every consumer (some ORMs/tools read pg_attribute.attnotnull); this step sets the real attribute so those consumers see it afterward
versionNotes: Scan-skip added in PostgreSQL 12 (SET NOT NULL uses a suitable valid CHECK constraint to avoid the verification scan)
PG 18 extends the idea: SET NOT NULL can also be satisfied by a valid not-null constraint, and will VALIDATE an existing invalid not-null constraint (a scan) if that is the only proof

title: Step 4 (optional) — DROP the now-redundant proving CHECK constraint
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP CONSTRAINT t_col_not_null;
safeRewrite: Run as-is with a short lock_timeout + retry. Only drop AFTER SET NOT NULL has committed — dropping in the same statement as SET NOT NULL defeats the scan-skip. Once the real NOT NULL attribute is set, the CHECK is pure overhead (extra per-write predicate eval + catalog clutter).
safeWhen: SET NOT NULL has already committed (the column attribute now enforces non-null independently)
Guarded by lock_timeout + retry
unsafeWhen: Combining DROP CONSTRAINT into the same command as SET NOT NULL (scan not skipped)
Dropping before SET NOT NULL committed — removes the only proof and forces a scan on the pending SET NOT NULL
edgeCases: Purely metadata; no scan, no rewrite; but still ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and thus lock-queue sensitive
Keeping the CHECK is harmless correctness-wise and some teams leave it; the tradeoff is a small per-write predicate cost and duplicate enforcement
versionNotes: Behavior consistent across all supported versions

title: ALTER COLUMN ... DROP NOT NULL
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN col DROP NOT NULL;
safeRewrite: Run as-is, guarded by a short lock_timeout + retry. DROP NOT NULL is always a pure catalog change — it never scans and never rewrites — so the only risk is the brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock and the lock queue behind it, not dwell time.
safeWhen: Always cost-safe (no scan/rewrite) as long as the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE acquisition is bounded by lock_timeout + retry
unsafeWhen: No lock_timeout on a hot table — the momentary ACCESS EXCLUSIVE can still trigger the lock-queue pileup if it has to wait behind a long query
Column is part of a PRIMARY KEY (rejected — PK columns are implicitly NOT NULL)
On a partition, the column is marked NOT NULL in the parent (must DROP NOT NULL on the parent instead)
edgeCases: Docs: 'If this table is a partition, one cannot perform DROP NOT NULL on a column if it is marked NOT NULL in the parent table. To drop the NOT NULL constraint from all the partitions, perform DROP NOT NULL on the parent table.'
PG 18: NOT NULL constraints are real pg_constraint rows; DROP NOT NULL removes the constraint object. If the column is also referenced by a replica identity or is the PK, the drop is blocked
Dependent views/functions are unaffected — no data or rowtype change, so no dependency rebuild
versionNotes: Always metadata-only in all supported versions
PG 18 models NOT NULL as pg_constraint (contype 'n'); DROP NOT NULL / DROP CONSTRAINT both remove it, but PK-implied NOT NULL still cannot be dropped

title: PG 18+ native — ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT NULL ... NOT VALID then VALIDATE (replaces the CHECK trick)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT t_col_nn NOT NULL col NOT VALID;  -- then, separately:  ALTER TABLE t VALIDATE CONSTRAINT t_col_nn;
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (ADD ... NOT VALID) then SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (VALIDATE)
safeRewrite: On PG 18+, prefer this native two-step over the CHECK-constraint dance: (1) ADD ... NOT NULL ... NOT VALID (METADATA_ONLY, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, brief — guard with lock_timeout+retry); (2) VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (SCAN, SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, online, does not block reads/writes). The column becomes a fully valid NOT NULL with no separate SET NOT NULL and no leftover redundant CHECK to drop.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 18 or newer
Steps run as two separate transactions (NOT VALID committed before VALIDATE)
ADD step guarded by lock_timeout + retry
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL 17 or older — NOT VALID is not accepted for not-null constraints; you must use the CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID + VALIDATE + SET NOT NULL sequence instead
Collapsing ADD (without NOT VALID) and VALIDATE into one path — a plain ADD ... NOT NULL scans under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
edgeCases: Docs: NOT VALID 'is currently only allowed for foreign-key, CHECK, and not-null constraints' (PG 18)
An invalid (NOT VALID) not-null constraint still enforces on new writes but is not yet trusted by the planner; the column's pg_attribute.attnotnull reflects a valid not-null only after VALIDATE

Docs: 'If a column has an invalid not-null constraint, SET NOT NULL validates it' — so a stray SET NOT NULL on such a column triggers the full scan under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE; validate via VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE) instead
cost is CONDITIONAL because the expensive part (the scan) is isolated in VALIDATE (online); the ADD itself is metadata-only
versionNotes: Native NOT NULL ... NOT VALID / VALIDATE CONSTRAINT for not-null constraints introduced in PostgreSQL 18; not-null constraints are catalogued in pg_constraint
For PG 12–17, use the CHECK-constraint fast path (entries add-check-not-null-not-valid → validate-check-constraint → set-not-null-fastpath → drop-redundant-check)
_correction: The ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT NULL ... NOT VALID step holds a momentary ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock, so it briefly blocks reads AND writes (matching add-check-not-null-not-valid). Only the VALIDATE step (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE) is non-blocking.

title: ALTER TABLE ... DROP COLUMN (plain, no dependents) — metadata-only catalog change under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN [IF EXISTS] col;
safeWhen: No application code path references the column anymore (no SELECT *, no explicit reference, ORM told to ignore it and that release is fully deployed to all instances/replicas)
The statement runs with a short lock_timeout (commonly < 2s) so it cannot sit in the lock queue and stall reads/writes
No long-running transaction or idle-in-transaction session is holding a conflicting lock (even ACCESS SHARE) on the table at execution time
Column has no dependent objects outside the table, OR its only dependents are indexes/constraints on the same table that you intend to drop with it
unsafeWhen: Application still issues SELECT * (or an ORM/driver with a cached table schema) — running instances raise errors (missing column, or the prepared-statement error 'cached plan must not change result type') until they reconnect/reboot
Run with no lock_timeout behind a long query: the queued ACCESS EXCLUSIVE blocks ALL new reads and writes on the table for the duration of that query

Column is referenced by a view, foreign key from another table, generated column, trigger, or default/check expression and you have not planned for it (plain RESTRICT will error; CASCADE would silently destroy them)
safeRewrite: 1) In application code, stop referencing the column: eliminate SELECT * on the table and add the column to the ORM ignore list (e.g. Rails self.ignored_columns += ['col']). 2) Deploy that code to every app instance and replica. 3) Run the drop in its own short transaction with SET lock_timeout = '2s' (retry with backoff on failure): SET lock_timeout='2s'; ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN col; 4) After success, remove the ignore-list entry in a later release.
edgeCases: DROP COLUMN does NOT physically remove data: the attribute is flagged attisdropped in pg_attribute and the column is only made invisible; on-disk row size is unchanged until each row is later rewritten (docs: 'dropping a column is quick but it will not immediately reduce the on-disk size of your table').
Space is reclaimed only gradually as rows are UPDATEd, or immediately only via a full-table rewrite (VACUUM FULL / pg_repack / a rewriting ALTER form) — see the space-reclamation entry.

Prepared statements / cached plans that projected the column (typ. SELECT *) are invalidated; on next execution PostgreSQL raises 'cached plan must not change result type' until the session re-prepares — a real production symptom of the schema-cache-breakage class.
The dropped column's out-of-line TOAST values are also not reclaimed until row rewrite (see TOAST entry).
IF EXISTS only suppresses the 'column does not exist' error; it does not change locking or dependency behavior.
versionNotes: Behavior is stable across all currently supported majors (14–18); wording verified against postgresql.org/docs/current (v18).
lock_timeout is available since PostgreSQL 9.3 — the safe-acquisition recipe assumes it.
Braintree/Paul Gross list 'Drop a column' under safe operations, but that predates the modern emphasis on the app-schema-cache caveat that strong_migrations codifies.

title: Acquiring the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock — the lock-queue hazard that blocks reads and writes
sqlPattern: SET lock_timeout='2s'; ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN col;  -- (contrast: same statement with no lock_timeout)
safeWhen: A short lock_timeout is set (values < 2s are common) so the statement aborts rather than parking an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request in the queue
There are no long-running transactions, analytics queries, or idle-in-transaction sessions on the table (run heavy/analytic reads on replicas)
Failed acquisitions are retried with exponential backoff rather than left to block
unsafeWhen: No lock_timeout and a conflicting lock is held: the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request queues, and because it conflicts with every lock mode, ALL subsequent queries — including plain SELECTs needing only ACCESS SHARE — queue BEHIND it, so the table appears frozen for reads and writes even though the DDL itself would be instant
An idle-in-transaction connection holds a lock indefinitely, so 'a transaction seeking a lock will wait indefinitely for conflicting locks to be released'
safeRewrite: Wrap the drop so it can only ever hold the lock briefly and never poison the queue: begin a dedicated transaction/session, SET lock_timeout to a small value (e.g. '2s'), attempt the ALTER TABLE, and on lock_timeout error retry with exponential backoff. Before deploying, enable log_lock_waits and log_min_duration_statement to surface long queries, and move long reads to replicas.
edgeCases: The cost is CONDITIONAL: the DDL work is metadata-only (~constant), but total dwell time = time waiting for the lock, which is unbounded if a long query or idle-in-transaction session is present.
This hazard is identical for any ACCESS EXCLUSIVE ALTER; it is the single most common cause of a 'fast' migration taking down a table.
Diagnose an in-progress stall via pg_stat_activity (waiting sessions) joined to pg_locks (holder / granted=false); idle-in-transaction holders often show no active query.
versionNotes: lock_timeout requires PostgreSQL ≥ 9.3.
Queue semantics (a queued ACCESS EXCLUSIVE blocking conflicting requests behind it) are unchanged across all supported majors.

title: DROP COLUMN RESTRICT (default) with dependent views / foreign keys
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN col [RESTRICT];
safeWhen: You WANT a hard failure if anything outside the table depends on the column — RESTRICT is the safe default because it refuses rather than silently cascading
Column has no external dependents (views, foreign keys from other tables, functions/rules referencing it)
unsafeWhen: You react to the RESTRICT error by switching to CASCADE without enumerating what will be destroyed (see cascade entry)
You assume RESTRICT protects same-table objects: indexes and table constraints ON this table are still dropped automatically even under RESTRICT
safeRewrite: Keep RESTRICT (the default). If it errors with a dependency, enumerate dependents explicitly (query pg_depend / \d+, information_schema.view_column_usage, and constraint catalogs), then drop or migrate each dependent in a controlled, reviewed order — do not reach for CASCADE blindly.
edgeCases: Docs: RESTRICT means 'Refuse to drop the column ... if there are any dependent objects. This is the default behavior.'

Same-table indexes and table constraints 'involving the column will be automatically dropped' even with RESTRICT — RESTRICT only guards objects OUTSIDE the table ('You will need to say CASCADE if anything outside the table depends on the column, for example, foreign key references or views').
Multivariate (extended) statistics referencing the column are dropped if removing the column would leave the statistics object with only one column.
versionNotes: RESTRICT/CASCADE semantics for DROP COLUMN are stable across all supported majors.

title: DROP COLUMN ... CASCADE — silent destruction of dependent views and cross-table constraints
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN col CASCADE;
safeWhen: You have already enumerated every dependent object and confirmed each SHOULD be dropped
The dependents are trivial and known (e.g. a single throwaway view you intend to recreate)
unsafeWhen: Used reflexively to get past a RESTRICT error: CASCADE 'automatically drop[s] objects that depend on the dropped column ... and in turn all objects that depend on those objects' — you can lose views, materialized views, rules, functions, and foreign-key constraints without a second prompt

The column is referenced by a foreign key from another table: dropping that FK constraint requires and briefly takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the OTHER table too, extending the blast radius and lock footprint to tables the migration never named
safeRewrite: Never let CASCADE run unreviewed. First list dependents (WITH RECURSIVE over pg_depend, or test in a transaction: BEGIN; ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN col CASCADE; then read the NOTICEs listing dropped objects; ROLLBACK). Then drop/migrate each dependent explicitly in its own reviewed step, and only afterward drop the column with plain RESTRICT.
edgeCases: CASCADE cascades transitively: dropping a view can drop views/rules built on that view.
Dependent foreign-key constraints live on other tables; their removal takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on those tables, so a single DROP COLUMN CASCADE can momentarily lock multiple tables.
PostgreSQL emits NOTICE lines naming each cascaded drop — capture them; they are the only record of what was destroyed.
versionNotes: CASCADE semantics stable across all supported majors.

title: DROP COLUMN with indexes on the column — auto-dropped indexes and leftover INVALID indexes
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN col;  -- col participates in one or more indexes
safeWhen: You accept that every index whose definition includes the column will be dropped entirely (a multi-column index is dropped as a whole, not merely stripped of the column)
No query plan critically depends on a to-be-dropped index that also covers other columns
unsafeWhen: A multi-column index (or a covering/INCLUDE index) that also serves other predicates includes the dropped column: it disappears with the column, potentially regressing unrelated query plans with no separate warning

A prior CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on the column failed and left an INVALID index behind: DROP COLUMN removes it as part of the cascade, but if you instead planned to reuse/rebuild it, that intent is silently lost
safeRewrite: Before dropping, inventory indexes on the column (SELECT indexrelid::regclass, indexdef FROM pg_indexes / pg_index WHERE the column is a key or INCLUDE column; check indisvalid to catch leftover INVALID indexes from failed CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY). Recreate any still-needed composite index on the remaining columns first (CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY), then drop the column.
edgeCases: Docs: 'Indexes and table constraints involving the column will be automatically dropped as well.'

A failed CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY leaves an INVALID index (indisvalid=false) that still consumes space and must otherwise be dropped manually; a DROP COLUMN that includes it in an index will clean it up as a side effect.
Dropping the column also drops UNIQUE / PRIMARY KEY / EXCLUSION constraints defined on it; a primary-key drop can in turn affect replication identity.
versionNotes: INCLUDE (covering) indexes exist since PostgreSQL 11; the whole-index-drop behavior applies to them too.
Auto-drop-of-dependent-indexes behavior is stable across supported majors.

title: Reclaiming space after DROP COLUMN — heap and TOAST are not freed until a rewrite
sqlPattern:
-- after ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN col;  then one of:
VACUUM FULL t;   -- or:  pg_repack -t t   -- or a rewriting ALTER form
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (VACUUM FULL / rewriting ALTER); brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE only at swap (pg_repack)
safeWhen: Reclamation is genuinely needed (large column, significant dead space / bloat) and you can tolerate a full rewrite window — or you use pg_repack to avoid a long hold
Run during a maintenance window with a short lock_timeout guarding the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE acquisition
unsafeWhen: You run VACUUM FULL on a hot table to reclaim the dropped column's space: it holds ACCESS EXCLUSIVE for the entire rewrite (dwell scales with table BYTES), blocking all reads and writes for the duration
You assume DROP COLUMN alone shrank the table or freed TOAST storage — it does not
safeRewrite: If space must be reclaimed online, use pg_repack (rebuilds into a new heap and TOAST, taking ACCESS EXCLUSIVE only for a brief final swap) instead of VACUUM FULL. Otherwise schedule VACUUM FULL in a maintenance window with a short lock_timeout. If reclamation is not urgent, do nothing — space is recovered gradually as rows are UPDATEd.
edgeCases: Docs: 'the space occupied by the dropped column is not reclaimed. The space will be reclaimed over time as existing rows are updated'; immediate reclamation requires 'one of the forms of ALTER TABLE that performs a rewrite of the whole table ... reconstructing each row with the dropped column replaced by a null value.'
TOAST: out-of-line values that belonged to the dropped column remain in the TOAST relation until each row is rewritten; DROP COLUMN does not truncate the TOAST table.
Plain VACUUM (non-FULL) does NOT reclaim this space to the OS or rewrite rows — only a full rewrite (VACUUM FULL / pg_repack / rewriting ALTER) does.
pganalyze/Braintree note pg_repack as the standard online alternative to VACUUM FULL for high-volume tables.
versionNotes: Non-reclamation-until-rewrite behavior is stable across all supported majors.
pg_repack is an external extension, not core; verify version compatibility with the server major.

title: ADD FOREIGN KEY (validated, default single-step)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE child ADD CONSTRAINT fk FOREIGN KEY (parent_id) REFERENCES parent (id);
lockMode: SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE on BOTH the child (altered) table and the parent (referenced) table (PostgreSQL 9.5+). Before 9.5: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on both.
safeRewrite: Split into two steps: (1) ALTER TABLE child ADD CONSTRAINT fk FOREIGN KEY (parent_id) REFERENCES parent (id) NOT VALID; then in a SEPARATE transaction (2) ALTER TABLE child VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk;. Run each under a short lock_timeout (<2s) with retry/backoff.
blocksReadsWritesNote: SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE does not conflict with ACCESS SHARE (SELECT) or ROW SHARE, so reads proceed; it conflicts with ROW EXCLUSIVE, so INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on BOTH tables are blocked for the full duration of the validating scan.
safeWhen: Table is tiny/empty (scan and write-block are negligible)
Run in a maintenance window where write-blocking both tables is acceptable
unsafeWhen: Child table is large — the validating scan holds the write lock for the whole scan
Parent (referenced) table is a hot/high-traffic table — it too is write-locked for the duration
Any long-running transaction holds a conflicting lock, forcing the DDL to wait and stacking all writers behind it in the lock queue
edgeCases: The referenced columns must be a PRIMARY KEY or have a UNIQUE constraint/index; otherwise the ADD fails.

No index is auto-created on the referencing (child) columns — validation and later cascading deletes/updates degrade to sequential scans without one. Add the child-side index first (CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY).
Locking BOTH tables surprises people: a small child table can still stall writes on a large hot parent for the whole scan.

If the ALTER waits behind a long transaction, its SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE request sits in the lock queue and every subsequent writer queues behind it (reads still pass since SRE does not conflict with ACCESS SHARE).
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 9.5+: ADD FOREIGN KEY reduced to SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE (per current ALTER TABLE docs and Braintree).
Before 9.5: ADD FOREIGN KEY took ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on both tables, additionally blocking reads (documented behavior reflected in the older GoCardless post).

title: ADD FOREIGN KEY ... NOT VALID (step 1 of the safe two-step)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE child ADD CONSTRAINT fk FOREIGN KEY (parent_id) REFERENCES parent (id) NOT VALID;
lockMode: SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE on BOTH the child (altered) and parent (referenced) tables. Same lock strength as the validated form, but held only momentarily because the row-scan is skipped.
safeRewrite: This IS the safe first step. Immediately follow (in a separate transaction) with ALTER TABLE child VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk;. Wrap in lock_timeout (<2s) + retry so the brief write-lock cannot stack the lock queue behind a long transaction.
safeWhen: Almost always the preferred way to introduce an FK on a live large table
New rows are immediately enforced against the constraint even before VALIDATE runs
unsafeWhen: A long-running transaction holds a conflicting lock on either table: the momentary SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE request queues and blocks all writers behind it until it acquires or times out
The parent table is extremely hot and cannot tolerate even a sub-second write stall without a lock_timeout guard
edgeCases: NOT VALID marks the constraint convalidated=false; new INSERT/UPDATE on the child are enforced immediately, but pre-existing rows are NOT checked until VALIDATE.
Still requires the parent's referenced columns to be UNIQUE/PK.
Still acquires the lock on the parent table too — 'NOT VALID' removes the scan, not the parent-table lock.
Leaving the constraint permanently NOT VALID means the planner cannot use it for optimizations and existing bad rows remain unenforced.
versionNotes: NOT VALID for foreign keys available since PostgreSQL 9.1.
SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE lock strength for the ADD applies on 9.5+ (ACCESS EXCLUSIVE before 9.5).

title: VALIDATE CONSTRAINT for a foreign key (step 2 of the safe two-step)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE child VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk;
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the child (altered) table PLUS ROW SHARE on the parent (referenced) table.
safeRewrite: This IS the safe second step and needs no rewrite. Per the docs: 'validation acquires only a SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock on the table being altered. (If the constraint is a foreign key then a ROW SHARE lock is also required on the table referenced by the constraint.)' Reads and writes proceed concurrently during the scan.
safeWhen: Standard for validating a previously-added NOT VALID FK on a live table
SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE does not conflict with ROW EXCLUSIVE, so concurrent INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE continue; ACCESS SHARE reads continue too
unsafeWhen: A concurrent autovacuum/VACUUM, ANALYZE, CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, or another SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE DDL is running on the child — these conflict with SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE and the VALIDATE will wait (and queue writers behind it while it waits to acquire)
Only one SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE operation can hold the table at a time (it self-conflicts)
edgeCases: The scan visits every child row and probes the parent's unique index once per row; without an index on the referencing (child) columns this is slow but still non-blocking.
SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE self-conflicts and conflicts with SHARE/SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE/EXCLUSIVE/ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — it can be blocked by, or block, autovacuum on that table.
Even though it doesn't block DML once held, acquiring the lock still requires reaching the front of the lock queue; guard with lock_timeout.
If any pre-existing child row violates the FK, VALIDATE fails and the constraint stays NOT VALID — clean up orphan rows before retrying.
versionNotes: VALIDATE CONSTRAINT acquiring SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (rather than ACCESS EXCLUSIVE) is the documented current behavior; the reduced validation lock has been present since the early NOT VALID/VALIDATE work (FK NOT VALID since 9.1).

title: ADD CHECK CONSTRAINT (validated, default single-step)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT c CHECK (price > 0);
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the altered table.
safeRewrite: Split into two steps: (1) ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT c CHECK (price > 0) NOT VALID; then in a SEPARATE transaction (2) ALTER TABLE t VALIDATE CONSTRAINT c;. Guard step 1 with a short lock_timeout + retry.
safeWhen: Tiny/empty table where the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE scan is instantaneous
Maintenance window where a full read+write block is acceptable
unsafeWhen: Any non-trivial live table — ACCESS EXCLUSIVE conflicts with every lock mode including ACCESS SHARE, so it blocks reads AND writes for the entire verifying scan
The ALTER must wait behind a long transaction: the queued ACCESS EXCLUSIVE blocks ALL subsequent operations on the table, including plain SELECTs, until it resolves
edgeCases: Unlike ADD FOREIGN KEY, ADD CHECK is NOT one of the reduced-lock ALTER forms — it takes full ACCESS EXCLUSIVE even though it touches only one table.
strong_migrations flags this as blocking reads AND writes 'while every row is checked'.
Dependent views/rules on the table don't change the lock, but the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock will block queries through those views too.
versionNotes: NOT VALID for CHECK constraints available since PostgreSQL 9.2 (enables the safe rewrite).
The ACCESS EXCLUSIVE requirement for the single-step ADD CHECK is current documented behavior.

title: ADD CHECK CONSTRAINT ... NOT VALID (step 1 of the safe two-step)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT c CHECK (price > 0) NOT VALID;
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the altered table, but held only momentarily (the verifying scan is skipped).
safeRewrite: This IS the safe first step. Because the scan is skipped it holds ACCESS EXCLUSIVE only briefly — but that lock still conflicts with everything, so wrap it in lock_timeout (<2s) + retry/backoff to avoid stacking the lock queue. Follow with ALTER TABLE t VALIDATE CONSTRAINT c; in a separate transaction.
safeWhen: Preferred way to add a CHECK to a large live table
Guarded by a short lock_timeout so the brief exclusive lock cannot freeze the table if it has to wait
unsafeWhen: Run without lock_timeout while a long transaction holds any lock on the table: the queued ACCESS EXCLUSIVE blocks all reads and writes until acquired — the classic lock-queue outage even though the operation itself is fast
Extremely latency-sensitive tables where even a sub-second full block is unacceptable without careful timing
edgeCases: It is still ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — 'NOT VALID' removes the scan, not the lock strength (contrast with FK NOT VALID which is only SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE).
New INSERT/UPDATE are enforced against the CHECK immediately; pre-existing rows are unchecked until VALIDATE.
A permanently-NOT VALID CHECK is not usable by the planner (e.g. for constraint exclusion / SET NOT NULL proof) until validated.
versionNotes: CHECK ... NOT VALID available since PostgreSQL 9.2.

title: VALIDATE CONSTRAINT for a CHECK constraint (step 2 of the safe two-step)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t VALIDATE CONSTRAINT c;
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the altered table (no second table involved for a CHECK).
safeRewrite: This IS the safe second step; no rewrite needed. Per the docs the validation 'does not need to lock out concurrent updates' and 'acquires only a SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock on the table being altered.' The scan checks only pre-existing rows.
safeWhen: Standard for validating a previously-added NOT VALID CHECK on a live table
Concurrent reads (ACCESS SHARE) and writes (ROW EXCLUSIVE) both continue — neither conflicts with SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE
unsafeWhen: Concurrent autovacuum/VACUUM, ANALYZE, CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, or another SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE DDL on the same table conflicts and forces waiting
A pre-existing row violates the CHECK — VALIDATE aborts and the constraint stays NOT VALID
edgeCases: Full sequential scan of the table; dwell scales with row count (not bytes), reading heap tuples but not rewriting them.
TOAST: if the CHECK expression references a TOASTed/out-of-line column, each such value is de-TOASTed during the scan, increasing cost — but it is still non-blocking.
SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE self-conflicts and interacts with autovacuum; guard acquisition with lock_timeout even though it doesn't block DML once held.
If VALIDATE fails on a bad row, clean the data and re-run; the constraint remains enforced for new writes throughout.
versionNotes: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE validation lock is current documented behavior; CHECK NOT VALID/VALIDATE workflow available since PostgreSQL 9.2.

title: Adding NOT NULL via a CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) constraint, with PG12 SET NOT NULL scan-skip
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT c CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID;  -- then VALIDATE; then (PG12+) ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN col SET NOT NULL;
