lockMode: Step A ADD CHECK ... NOT VALID: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (brief). Step B VALIDATE CONSTRAINT: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (non-blocking scan). Step C (PG12+) SET NOT NULL: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (brief, scan skipped if a valid proving CHECK exists).
safeRewrite: Four-step pattern to add a real NOT NULL without a blocking full-table scan: (1) ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT c CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID; (2) clean up / confirm no NULLs; (3) ALTER TABLE t VALIDATE CONSTRAINT c; (non-blocking scan); (4) on PostgreSQL 12+, ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN col SET NOT NULL; — the docs state 'if a valid CHECK constraint is found which proves no NULL can exist, then the table scan is skipped', so SET NOT NULL only takes a brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE with no scan. Optionally then DROP the now-redundant CHECK. Guard each ACCESS EXCLUSIVE step with lock_timeout + retry.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12+: with the validated CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) in place, SET NOT NULL skips the scan and is a brief metadata-only lock — reads/writes blocked only momentarily
You want a genuine column NOT NULL (planner-usable, catalog attnotnull) rather than only a CHECK
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL 11 and earlier: SET NOT NULL always performs a full-table verifying scan under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, blocking reads and writes for the whole scan — there is no scan-skip, so stopping at the validated CHECK constraint is the only non-blocking option on those versions
Adding SET NOT NULL directly (no proving CHECK) on any version: full ACCESS EXCLUSIVE scan blocking reads and writes
edgeCases: The CHECK must be VALID (not NOT VALID) for PG12+ to trust it and skip the SET NOT NULL scan; a still-NOT VALID CHECK does not enable the optimization.
The proving CHECK must be exactly a NOT NULL proof on that column (e.g. col IS NOT NULL); a more complex predicate may not be recognized as proving non-nullness.
After SET NOT NULL succeeds you may DROP the redundant CHECK, but keeping it is harmless.
On PG11-, if you cannot take a maintenance window, keep the enforcement as the validated CHECK constraint rather than converting to a column NOT NULL.
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 12+: SET NOT NULL skips the table scan when a valid CHECK proves no NULLs (per ALTER TABLE docs and Braintree).
PostgreSQL 11 and earlier: SET NOT NULL always scans under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (no scan-skip).
CHECK ... NOT VALID available since 9.2; strong_migrations recommends the CHECK-constraint route to avoid the rewrite/scan.

title: Phase 1 — CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (build the backing index)
sqlPattern: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_tbl_col ON tbl (col);
safeRewrite: CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_tbl_col ON tbl (col);  -- run OUTSIDE any transaction block; must be its own autocommit statement. Then verify validity before proceeding: SELECT indexrelid::regclass FROM pg_index WHERE indrelid = 'tbl'::regclass AND NOT indisvalid;
blocksWritesDetail: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE does not conflict with ROW EXCLUSIVE (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) or ACCESS SHARE (SELECT), so concurrent reads and writes proceed. Per docs: 'PostgreSQL will build the index without taking any locks that prevent concurrent inserts, updates, or deletes on the table.'
safeWhen: This is the safe way to build the backing index for a later ADD CONSTRAINT ... USING INDEX; it is the recommended Phase 1 in strong_migrations, Braintree/pgrs, and the PostgreSQL docs.
Run as its own statement with autocommit — no surrounding transaction.
Table is a plain (non-partitioned) table.
unsafeWhen: Run inside an explicit transaction block or a migration framework that wraps DDL in a transaction — CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot run in a transaction block and will error.

Target is a partitioned table — concurrent builds on partitioned tables are not supported; you must build CONCURRENTLY on each partition, create the parent index with ON ONLY, then ATTACH PARTITION each child index.
Another concurrent index build is already running on the same table — only one concurrent build per table at a time.
costClassDetail: Two full heap scans plus waits for concurrent transactions to drain. Dwell time scales with row count (SCAN), and it takes longer than a standard build, but it never blocks reads or writes — the cost is duration/IO, not availability.
edgeCases: FAILURE LEAVES AN INVALID INDEX: a deadlock or a uniqueness violation aborts the command but leaves an 'invalid' index behind that is ignored for queries yet still carries update overhead. Recovery: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY then retry, or REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY.

UNIQUENESS ENFORCED EARLY: for a UNIQUE index, the constraint is already enforced against other transactions once the second scan begins — violations can surface in other sessions before the index is usable, or even if the build ultimately fails. A leftover invalid unique index KEEPS enforcing uniqueness until dropped.
Always confirm indisvalid = true before Phase 2 — feeding an INVALID index to ADD CONSTRAINT ... USING INDEX is a correctness trap.
TOAST: the index covers only the indexed key columns; wide/TOASTed non-key data does not add heap-rewrite cost here (no rewrite occurs).
versionNotes: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY available since 8.2; behavior described is stable across all currently supported majors (14–18).
REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY (an alternative recovery for an invalid index) requires PostgreSQL 12+.

title: Phase 2a — ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT ... UNIQUE USING INDEX
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT uq_tbl_col UNIQUE USING INDEX idx_tbl_col;
safeRewrite:
SET lock_timeout = '2s';  -- fail fast rather than queue behind / ahead of traffic
ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT uq_tbl_col UNIQUE USING INDEX idx_tbl_col;  -- retry with backoff on lock_timeout
safeWhen: The named index already exists and is VALID (indisvalid = true), was built by CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, and is a b-tree with default sort ordering, non-partial, no expression columns.

Because the index is pre-built, this is a catalog-only operation: no table scan, no rewrite. The ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock is held only for the brief duration of the catalog update — 'In all other cases, this is a fast operation' (docs).
Wrapped in lock_timeout + retry so the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock cannot sit in the lock queue and stall traffic.
unsafeWhen: No lock_timeout set: even though the operation itself is fast, if it cannot acquire ACCESS EXCLUSIVE immediately (e.g. a long-running SELECT or autovacuum holds a conflicting lock) it QUEUES, and because ACCESS EXCLUSIVE conflicts with every lock mode, all subsequent SELECTs/writes queue behind it — the table is effectively blocked for reads and writes until it completes (GoCardless, Xata/pgroll, depesz).
The index is INVALID or was left behind by a failed concurrent build.
A constraint of the same name already exists.
costClassDetail: METADATA_ONLY: creates the pg_constraint row and marks the existing index as constraint-owned. No heap scan, no rewrite. Dwell is ~constant regardless of table size — the risk is purely the lock queue, not duration.
edgeCases: If a constraint_name is given, the index is RENAMED to match it; if omitted, the constraint takes the index's name. Downstream tooling that references the old index name will break.
After this runs the index is 'owned' by the constraint — DROP CONSTRAINT will drop the index too. You can no longer DROP INDEX it independently.
DEFERRABLE / INITIALLY DEFERRED may be appended in the same clause.
Not supported on partitioned tables — 'This form is not currently supported on partitioned tables.'
versionNotes: ADD CONSTRAINT ... UNIQUE USING INDEX available since PostgreSQL 9.1; behavior stable through 18.
Lock-queue hazard and lock_timeout mitigation apply to all versions.

title: Phase 2b — ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT ... PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT pk_tbl PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX idx_tbl_col;
safeRewrite:
-- Step A (do FIRST, separately): make the key column(s) provably NOT NULL WITHOUT a blocking scan
ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT tbl_col_not_null CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID;  -- instant, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE but no scan
ALTER TABLE tbl VALIDATE CONSTRAINT tbl_col_not_null;                                 -- SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, does NOT block reads/writes
ALTER TABLE tbl ALTER COLUMN col SET NOT NULL;                                        -- PG12+: skips the scan because the validated CHECK proves no nulls
-- Step B: attach the PK to the pre-built valid unique index, under lock_timeout+retry
SET lock_timeout = '2s';
ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT pk_tbl PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX idx_tbl_col;
safeWhen: The key column(s) are ALREADY marked NOT NULL (or made so via the CHECK-constraint dance in Step A). Then ADD PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX is catalog-only (METADATA_ONLY) and the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock is held only briefly.
Backing index is a valid, non-partial, non-expression b-tree with default ordering built by CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY.
Wrapped in lock_timeout + retry.
unsafeWhen: The key column(s) are NOT already NOT NULL: 'If PRIMARY KEY is specified, and the index's columns are not already marked NOT NULL, then this command will attempt to do ALTER COLUMN SET NOT NULL against each such column. That requires a full table scan to verify the column(s) contain no nulls.' That scan runs WHILE HOLDING ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — reads and writes are blocked for the entire scan (dwell scales with rows). This is the silent trap that turns a 'fast' PK add into an outage.
No lock_timeout: same lock-queue hazard as the UNIQUE case.
costClassDetail: CONDITIONAL on the NOT NULL state of the key columns. If already NOT NULL → METADATA_ONLY (constant, brief). If not → the implicit SET NOT NULL forces a SCAN (full heap scan) under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, blocking reads and writes for the scan duration. Do the NOT NULL work first, separately, so Phase 2b degrades to METADATA_ONLY.
edgeCases: PG12+ SET NOT NULL scan-skip: a validated CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) lets SET NOT NULL avoid the scan ('Allow ALTER TABLE ... SET NOT NULL to avoid unnecessary table scans ... when the table's column constraints can be recognized as disallowing nulls'). On PG11 and earlier there is NO scan-skip — SET NOT NULL always scans, so budget for it.
After SET NOT NULL succeeds you may DROP the now-redundant CHECK constraint (metadata-only).
Multi-column PK: every key column needs the NOT NULL treatment.
Index becomes constraint-owned; dropping the PK drops the index.
Not supported on partitioned tables.
versionNotes: SET NOT NULL scan-avoidance via existing constraint: PostgreSQL 12+ only (commit bbb96c370).
On <=11 the NOT NULL path is unconditionally a full scan under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE.
ADD PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX itself: 9.1+.

title: Swap an existing PK — DROP CONSTRAINT + ADD PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX in one statement
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE distributors DROP CONSTRAINT distributors_pkey, ADD CONSTRAINT distributors_pkey PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX dist_id_temp_idx;
safeRewrite:
-- Ensure new key columns are NOT NULL first (see entry pg-uqpk-03 Step A), then:
SET lock_timeout = '2s';
ALTER TABLE distributors
  DROP CONSTRAINT distributors_pkey,
  ADD CONSTRAINT distributors_pkey PRIMARY KEY USING INDEX dist_id_temp_idx;  -- both sub-commands under ONE brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock; retry on lock_timeout
safeWhen: New key columns already NOT NULL, new index valid/concurrently-built, and NO foreign keys from other tables reference the old primary key.
Combining DROP + ADD in a single ALTER TABLE means one lock acquisition and no window where the table has no PK.
lock_timeout + retry in place.
unsafeWhen: Foreign keys in other tables reference the PK being dropped — DROP CONSTRAINT fails (or requires CASCADE, which would drop those FKs). Enumerate dependents first.
New key columns not yet NOT NULL → hidden full-scan SET NOT NULL under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (see pg-uqpk-03).
No lock_timeout → lock-queue stall.
costClassDetail: CONDITIONAL: METADATA_ONLY when new key columns are already NOT NULL and no dependents block the drop; escalates to SCAN if an implicit SET NOT NULL is triggered. The DROP side is catalog-only.
edgeCases: This is the exact pattern in the PostgreSQL docs for replacing a possibly-corrupt PK index without a long write block.
Dependent VIEWS/RULES referencing the table are unaffected by a PK swap (they depend on columns, not the constraint), but FKs referencing the PK are hard dependencies.
The old PK's index is dropped with its constraint; the new temp index is renamed to distributors_pkey and becomes constraint-owned.
Not supported on partitioned tables.
versionNotes: Pattern valid 9.1+; SET NOT NULL scan-skip caveat is PG12+ (see pg-uqpk-03).

title: ANTI-PATTERN — direct ADD CONSTRAINT ... UNIQUE / PRIMARY KEY (col) with no USING INDEX
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT uq_tbl_col UNIQUE (col);  -- or ADD PRIMARY KEY (col)
safeRewrite:
-- Do NOT add the constraint inline. Split into the two safe phases:
CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_tbl_col ON tbl (col);            -- Phase 1 (entry 01)
SET lock_timeout = '2s';
ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT uq_tbl_col UNIQUE USING INDEX idx_tbl_col;  -- Phase 2 (entry 02)
safeWhen: Only on a small or empty table, or a maintenance window where blocking all reads and writes for the full index-build duration is acceptable.
Inside a create-table / bulk-load transaction before the table is exposed to traffic.
unsafeWhen: Any high-volume / live table. 'In Postgres, adding a unique constraint creates a unique index, which blocks reads and writes' (strong_migrations). PostgreSQL builds the backing unique index NON-concurrently while holding ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, so both reads and writes are blocked for the entire build, and dwell scales with row count.
costClassDetail: SCAN: a full, standard (non-concurrent) unique index build under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE. Unlike the standard-CREATE-INDEX case (which blocks only writes), doing it via ADD CONSTRAINT takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and blocks reads too, for the whole build.
edgeCases: For PRIMARY KEY, the same statement also sets the columns NOT NULL — layering another scan on top.
A uniqueness violation aborts the whole ALTER TABLE at the end of the scan, after the lock was held the entire time — worst of both worlds.
This is the specific operation the safe two-phase rewrite exists to replace; a migration-safety tool should flag it.
versionNotes: True on all supported versions; there is no version where inline ADD UNIQUE/PRIMARY KEY builds its index concurrently.

title: Cleanup — DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY on a leftover INVALID index from a failed build
sqlPattern: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_tbl_col;
safeWhen: A prior CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY failed (deadlock / uniqueness violation) and left an invalid index; drop it before retrying.
Run outside a transaction block.
unsafeWhen: Run inside a transaction block — DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot run in one.
Plain DROP INDEX (non-concurrent) is used instead — that takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and briefly blocks reads/writes (still fast, but not lock-free).
costClassDetail: METADATA_ONLY and lock-light: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY takes only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE and does not block reads or writes.
edgeCases: CRITICAL for unique indexes: a leftover INVALID unique index KEEPS ENFORCING uniqueness against writers even though it is unusable for queries — it can reject otherwise-valid inserts. Drop it promptly.
If the invalid index was already promoted to constraint-owned (it cannot be, if invalid — ADD CONSTRAINT USING INDEX requires a valid index), use ALTER TABLE DROP CONSTRAINT instead.
An invalid index still incurs write/update maintenance overhead until dropped.
versionNotes: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY requires PostgreSQL 9.2+.
REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY (rebuild instead of drop) requires 12+.

title: Mitigation wrapper — lock_timeout + retry around the brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE Phase 2
sqlPattern: SET lock_timeout = '2s'; ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT ... USING INDEX ...;  -- retry loop on lock_timeout error
safeRewrite:
-- Application/migration-runner loop (pseudocode):
for attempt in 1..N:
  begin
    SET lock_timeout = '2s';        -- or a value < ~2s (Xata/pgroll common practice)
    ALTER TABLE tbl ADD CONSTRAINT ... USING INDEX ...;
    break
  on lock_timeout / '55P03':
    sleep(exponential_backoff(attempt)); continue
safeWhen: Always pair with the Phase 2 ADD CONSTRAINT ... USING INDEX statements. The operation itself is fast, but acquiring ACCESS EXCLUSIVE may not be — lock_timeout bounds how long the acquisition attempt can sit in the lock queue.
Retries use exponential backoff (pgroll pattern) so a transient blocker (long SELECT, autovacuum) doesn't fail the deploy outright.
unsafeWhen: Omitting lock_timeout: 'As AccessExclusive locks conflict with every other type of lock, having one sat in the queue blocks all other operations on that table' (GoCardless) — a single queued ADD CONSTRAINT behind a long query stalls every SELECT and write on the table until it clears.

Setting statement_timeout instead of lock_timeout for lock-acquisition control: statement_timeout also caps the useful work; lock_timeout specifically bounds the wait for the lock and is the correct tool here (depesz comments note lock_timeout is superior for this).
costClassDetail: The wrapped statement is METADATA_ONLY; the wrapper does not change its cost — it changes its FAILURE MODE from 'block the whole table' to 'abort fast and retry'. 'It's better to abort a deploy than take your application down' (GoCardless).
edgeCases: lock_timeout must be SET before the ALTER; SET LOCAL requires a transaction, but CREATE/DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot be in one — for those, set lock_timeout at session/role level instead.
Values under ~2s are common (Xata/pgroll).
Even with the lock held only ~milliseconds, queries that arrive during the wait will pause; keep the retry window short and off-peak.
Monitor blockers with pg_blocking_pids() rather than scanning pg_locks continuously (pganalyze).
versionNotes: lock_timeout available since PostgreSQL 9.3.
Guidance applies across all supported versions.

title: ALTER TABLE ... RENAME TO — rename a table
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE users RENAME TO users_old;
safeRewrite: Do not rename an in-use table in place. Use expand/contract with a view or dual-write: (1) CREATE the new table (or CREATE VIEW new_name AS SELECT * FROM old_name for read-compat, or the reverse), (2) deploy application code that reads/writes the new name, (3) dual-write / backfill if a physical move is required, (4) shift reads, (5) stop writing the old name, (6) DROP the old object in a separate migration. If an in-place rename is truly required, run it in a transaction with `SET LOCAL lock_timeout = '2s'` (or less) and retry with backoff so it aborts instead of stalling the lock queue, and deploy app code that already tolerates BOTH names before the rename.
safeWhen: Table is not referenced by any live application code, replication slot consumer, or scheduled job (e.g. a scratch/staging table you fully control).
Wrapped in a transaction with a short lock_timeout AND the application already accepts both the old and new name (name-agnostic deploy).
Table is small AND traffic is quiesced / in a maintenance window.
unsafeWhen: The table is referenced by name in currently-deployed application code, stored procedures, views, or prepared statements — those break the instant the catalog flips.

Run without lock_timeout against a busy table: an in-progress long transaction holding ACCESS SHARE forces the ALTER into the lock queue, and every subsequent query (including plain SELECTs) queues behind it, causing a full-table stall until the blocker finishes.
Foreign key names, sequence ownership, and index names are auto-derived from the table name in your tooling and are assumed stable downstream.
edgeCases: RENAME does NOT rename dependent objects' auto-generated names: sequences owned by SERIAL columns, indexes, and FK constraints keep their old name-derived identifiers, which can confuse later migrations and ORMs.
Dependent VIEWs, rules, and FK constraints are stored by OID, not name, so they keep working after the rename — but application SQL that hardcodes the table name does not.
'There is no effect on the stored data' (PG docs): no heap or index rewrite, so dwell time is constant regardless of table size or TOAST volume.
Same code path (RenameStmt / ACCESS EXCLUSIVE) applies to RENAME of a view, materialized view, sequence, or foreign table.
versionNotes: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE for table rename across all supported versions (14–18); no version has reduced it.
ACCESS EXCLUSIVE conflicts with all lock modes, so it is the only lock that blocks a plain SELECT (ACCESS SHARE).

title: ALTER TABLE ... RENAME COLUMN ... TO — rename a column
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE users RENAME COLUMN email TO email_address;
safeRewrite: Expand/contract: (1) ADD the new column (metadata-only for a NULL/constant default in PG 11+), (2) backfill in batches and dual-write via application code or a trigger, (3) shift reads to the new column, (4) stop writing the old column, (5) DROP the old column in a later migration. Never rename a column referenced by live code in a single step. If an in-place rename is unavoidable, gate it behind a deploy in which the app reads/writes both names, and run it with `SET LOCAL lock_timeout` + retry.
safeWhen: Column is not referenced by deployed code, views, generated columns, indexes-by-expression, or check constraints that name it.
Application layer has been deployed to tolerate both old and new column names before the rename runs, inside a short-lock_timeout transaction.
unsafeWhen: Column name appears in currently-running SQL (SELECT lists, INSERT column lists, ORM mappings, prepared statements) — those error immediately after the catalog change.
Executed without lock_timeout on a hot table: the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request queues behind any open transaction and then blocks all reads and writes to the table until it acquires and releases.
The column is used in a VIEW, CHECK constraint, index expression, or trigger body that references it by name and would need coordinated updates.
edgeCases: Views and constraints that reference the column are stored by attribute number, so they auto-follow the rename and keep working — but raw application SQL and prepared statements do not.
'There is no effect on the stored data' — no rewrite; constant time regardless of row count / TOAST.
On a partitioned table, RENAME COLUMN on the parent renames the column on all partitions and takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the parent and each child.
A concurrently-cached plan referencing the old name via a prepared statement will fail on next execution.
versionNotes: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE across all supported versions; not reduced in any release.

title: ALTER TABLE ... RENAME CONSTRAINT ... TO — rename a constraint
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE orders RENAME CONSTRAINT orders_pkey TO orders_primary_key;
safeRewrite: Purely cosmetic; only unsafe due to the lock queue and any tooling that keys off the constraint name. Run in a transaction with `SET LOCAL lock_timeout = '2s'` and retry with backoff. There is no dual-write path needed unless downstream tooling (migration frameworks, monitoring) references the old constraint name — in which case update that tooling first.
safeWhen: Executed with a short lock_timeout on a table that is not under a long-running transaction.
No external tooling, migration history, or code path depends on the constraint name string.
unsafeWhen: Run without lock_timeout on a busy table — same lock-queue stall as any ACCESS EXCLUSIVE DDL.
Migration frameworks or app code that reference the constraint by name (e.g. ON CONFLICT ON CONSTRAINT name, or error-message parsing) rely on the old name.
edgeCases: When renaming a constraint that has an underlying index (PRIMARY KEY / UNIQUE / EXCLUDE), 'the index is renamed as well' (PG docs) — this cascade happens under the same ALTER TABLE ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock, NOT the reduced ALTER INDEX RENAME lock.
`ON CONFLICT ON CONSTRAINT <name>` clauses in application code break if they reference the old name.
No stored-data effect; constant time.
versionNotes: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE across all supported versions. Note the asymmetry: renaming the index directly via ALTER INDEX takes the weaker SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (PG 12+), but renaming it via RENAME CONSTRAINT does not.

title: ALTER INDEX ... RENAME TO — rename an index
sqlPattern: ALTER INDEX idx_users_email RENAME TO idx_users_email_address;
safeRewrite: Generally safe as-is on PG 12+: 'Renaming an index acquires a SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock' (PG docs), which does NOT conflict with SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE, so reads and writes on the underlying table continue. Still wrap in a transaction with a short lock_timeout because SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE conflicts with other DDL, VACUUM, ANALYZE, and CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on the same relation. On PG <= 11, treat like the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE rename variants above.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12 or newer, where the reduced SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock lets DML proceed concurrently.
No concurrent VACUUM, ANALYZE, CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, REINDEX, or other ALTER on the same table (those share/conflict with SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE).
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL <= 11, where ALTER INDEX ... RENAME took ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and blocks reads and writes like any exclusive DDL.
Application code, monitoring, or migration tooling references the index by name (e.g. index hints via pg_hint_plan, or drift checks).
edgeCases: Because the lock is only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, two sessions renaming/altering the same index concurrently can interleave; keep index-DDL migrations serialized.
No heap or index rewrite — the b-tree/GiST/etc. structure is untouched; constant time.
Renaming an index does NOT rename the constraint that owns it; to rename a PK/UNIQUE constraint use RENAME CONSTRAINT (which takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE).
versionNotes: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE for ALTER INDEX ... RENAME since PostgreSQL 12; PostgreSQL 11 and earlier used ACCESS EXCLUSIVE.
Confirmed in current docs: 'Renaming an index acquires a SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock.'
_correction: CORRECTED (was inverted): ALTER INDEX ... RENAME DOES rename the owning UNIQUE/PK/EXCLUDE constraint as well, under SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE. The asymmetry: RENAME CONSTRAINT renames the index under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, whereas RENAME INDEX renames the constraint under SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE. Tooling keying off the constraint name can break even from ALTER INDEX RENAME.

title: ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... SET DEFAULT — set/change a column default
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN status SET DEFAULT 'active';
safeRewrite: The catalog change itself is safe and instant — 'The new default value will only apply in subsequent INSERT or UPDATE commands; it does not cause rows already in the table to change' (PG docs). It updates only pg_attrdef; no scan, no rewrite, no backfill, regardless of table size or default expression volatility. Run it in a transaction with `SET LOCAL lock_timeout = '2s'` and retry with backoff so the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request cannot stall the lock queue on a busy table. Do NOT assume existing rows pick up the new default — backfill separately in batches if needed.
safeWhen: Always metadata-only for an existing column, including with a volatile default like now() or gen_random_uuid() — the volatility only affects future inserts, not the ALTER cost.
Executed with a short lock_timeout so it aborts rather than blocking behind a long transaction.
unsafeWhen: Run without lock_timeout on a hot table: the brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock queues behind any open transaction and then blocks all reads and writes until it is acquired and released.
Operators expect existing rows to be backfilled with the new default — they are not; this is a correctness trap, not a lock trap.
edgeCases: Contrast with ADD COLUMN: ADD COLUMN ... DEFAULT <constant> is metadata-only since PostgreSQL 11 (stored in pg_attribute as a missing-value), but ADD COLUMN with a VOLATILE default still triggers a full REWRITE. SET DEFAULT on an already-existing column never rewrites.
DROP DEFAULT is the inverse and equivalent to SET DEFAULT NULL — same lock and cost.
On a partitioned table, SET DEFAULT on the parent sets it for the partitioned table's future inserts; child partitions can have their own defaults.
SET DEFAULT does not affect NOT NULL enforcement or existing NULLs; combining a default with adding NOT NULL is a separate, potentially scanning operation.
versionNotes: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE for SET DEFAULT across all supported versions (not in the reduced-lock list in the ALTER TABLE docs).
The PG 11 fast-default optimization applies to ADD COLUMN ... DEFAULT, not to SET DEFAULT on an existing column — but SET DEFAULT was already metadata-only in all versions.

title: ALTER TABLE ... ALTER COLUMN ... DROP DEFAULT — remove a column default
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE users ALTER COLUMN status DROP DEFAULT;
safeRewrite: Metadata-only and equivalent to SET DEFAULT NULL: 'removal is equivalent to setting the default value to NULL' (PG docs). It only removes the pg_attrdef entry; existing rows are untouched. Run in a transaction with a short lock_timeout + retry. The application risk is that INSERTs which previously omitted the column and relied on the server default will now insert NULL (or fail if the column is NOT NULL) — deploy code that supplies the value explicitly BEFORE dropping the default.
safeWhen: No live INSERT path relies on the server-side default for this column (all inserters supply the value explicitly).
Executed with a short lock_timeout to avoid stalling the lock queue.
unsafeWhen: Existing application code performs INSERTs that omit the column and depend on the default — after DROP DEFAULT those rows get NULL, or the INSERT errors if the column is NOT NULL.
Run without lock_timeout on a busy table (lock-queue stall, same as any ACCESS EXCLUSIVE DDL).
edgeCases: If the column is NOT NULL and an inserter omitted it relying on the default, DROP DEFAULT turns those inserts into constraint violations — an application-breaking change disguised as a metadata tweak.
No effect on stored rows; constant time.
For a SERIAL/identity-style column, dropping the default detaches the sequence default but does not drop the owned sequence.
versionNotes: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE across all supported versions; DROP DEFAULT is not in the reduced-lock list.

title: ALTER {VIEW|MATERIALIZED VIEW|SEQUENCE|FOREIGN TABLE} ... RENAME TO — rename other relations
sqlPattern: ALTER SEQUENCE users_id_seq RENAME TO users_identity_seq;  ALTER VIEW active_users RENAME TO active_users_v2;
safeRewrite: Same treatment as table rename: these are catalog-only but application-breaking. Prefer creating the new object and switching code over, or wrap the rename in a transaction with a short lock_timeout after deploying name-tolerant code. For sequences specifically, renaming a sequence owned by a SERIAL/identity column does not change the column's default expression's resolution (it is stored by OID), but nextval('name') calls and monitoring that hardcode the sequence name will break.
safeWhen: The relation is not referenced by name in live code (raw SQL, nextval('seq') calls, view names in queries).
Short lock_timeout used and app already tolerates both names.
unsafeWhen: Code calls nextval('old_seq_name') / currval(), or SELECTs the view/foreign table by its old name.
Run without lock_timeout on a relation under concurrent load.
edgeCases: A materialized view rename takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE but does not refresh or rewrite contents.
Column defaults that call nextval() reference the sequence by OID (regclass), so renaming the sequence does not break the default — only literal string references break.
Foreign table rename is catalog-only on the local side; it does not touch the remote server.
versionNotes: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE across all supported versions for these RENAME forms (shared RenameStmt path with table rename).

title: CREATE TABLE ... PARTITION OF (create a brand-new partition inline)
sqlPattern: CREATE TABLE measurement_y2026m07 PARTITION OF measurement FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-07-01') TO ('2026-08-01');
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the partitioned parent table; the new child is created and locked ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (it is brand new, so no external contention). If a DEFAULT partition exists, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the default partition too.
safeWhen: No DEFAULT partition exists (so no default-partition scan is triggered) AND you can take the brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the parent under a short lock_timeout with retries.
The parent is not under long-running transactions/queries, so the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock is granted quickly rather than queuing.
unsafeWhen: A DEFAULT partition exists: the default partition is scanned to prove no rows belong in the new bounds, and it is held under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE for the duration (cost scales with default-partition rows).

Any long-running query/txn holds a conflicting lock on the parent: the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request queues and every subsequent SELECT/DML on the whole partition hierarchy piles up behind it (lock-queue stampede).
safeRewrite: Prefer building the table standalone and using ATTACH PARTITION (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on parent, PG 12+) instead of CREATE TABLE ... PARTITION OF, which forces ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the parent. Always wrap in a session with a short lock_timeout (e.g. SET lock_timeout = '2s') and retry with backoff so a blocked create aborts instead of freezing the hierarchy. Avoid a DEFAULT partition, or attach a CHECK on the default that excludes the new range so its scan is skipped.
blocksReadsWritesNote: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the parent blocks reads and writes across the entire partition hierarchy while held; the operation itself is fast (metadata-only for an empty new child) but the lock acquisition is the risk.
costClassRationale: METADATA_ONLY for the new empty child, but SCAN of the default partition when one exists — hence CONDITIONAL.
edgeCases: CREATE TABLE ... PARTITION OF takes a stronger parent lock (ACCESS EXCLUSIVE) than ATTACH PARTITION (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, PG 12+); the docs explicitly recommend ATTACH for concurrency-friendliness.
Indexes/constraints defined on the parent are automatically created on the new partition as part of this DDL, extending the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE window if the child were non-empty (it is empty here).
If the partition key involves an expression and the partition does not accept NULLs, CHECK-based scan-skipping does not apply.
versionNotes: Declarative partitioning introduced in PostgreSQL 10.
DEFAULT partitions introduced in PostgreSQL 11.
Lock level on the parent for CREATE TABLE ... PARTITION OF remains ACCESS EXCLUSIVE across all supported versions (this is what ATTACH PARTITION was optimized away from in PG 12).

title: ATTACH PARTITION of a populated table WITHOUT a matching CHECK constraint (validation scan runs)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE measurement ATTACH PARTITION measurement_y2026m07 FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-07-01') TO ('2026-08-01');
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the partitioned parent (PG 12+), plus ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the table being attached (held while it is full-scanned to validate the partition constraint), plus ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the DEFAULT partition if one exists. SHARE on any tables that reference the parent via foreign keys.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12+ (parent lock is only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, which does NOT block SELECT/INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on existing partitions).
The table being attached is not receiving concurrent traffic in its standalone form (its ACCESS EXCLUSIVE + scan only blocks access to that not-yet-attached table).
No DEFAULT partition exists (or the default has a CHECK excluding the new bounds so it is not scanned).
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL 11 or older: ATTACH takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the PARENT, blocking all reads and writes to the whole hierarchy for the full scan duration.
The attached table is large and hot in standalone form: the validation full scan holds ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on it, dwell time scales with row count.
A DEFAULT partition exists and is large: it is scanned under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE to prove no rows must move, blocking access to the default partition for the scan duration.
SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE still conflicts with VACUUM/ANALYZE/other DDL — a blocked ATTACH queues behind autovacuum and then blocks subsequent conflicting DDL (but not DML).
safeRewrite: Add a matching CHECK constraint to the child BEFORE attaching so the validation scan is skipped: (1) ALTER TABLE child ADD CONSTRAINT ck CHECK (ts >= '2026-07-01' AND ts < '2026-08-01') NOT VALID; (2) ALTER TABLE child VALIDATE CONSTRAINT ck (scan, but only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, non-blocking to DML); (3) ATTACH PARTITION becomes metadata-only for the child. Add a NOT NULL on the key for list partitions that reject NULL. Always set a short lock_timeout with retries. See the 'attach-partition-with-check' entry.
edgeCases: CHECK-skip does NOT work if a partition key is an expression and the partition does not accept NULLs; for list partitions add a NOT NULL constraint on the key column unless it is an expression.
If the attached table is itself partitioned, further locks are taken on all its sub-partitions (avoidable via CHECK per docs Section 5.12.2.2).

The child must have exactly the parent's columns (no extra), matching types, and all the parent's NOT NULL and CHECK constraints (not NO INHERIT); indexes and row-level triggers matching the parent are created/attached during ATTACH.
Foreign-table children cannot be attached if the parent has UNIQUE indexes.
versionNotes: PG 12+: parent lock reduced from ACCESS EXCLUSIVE to SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (commit making ATTACH concurrency-friendly).
PG 11 and earlier: ATTACH PARTITION holds ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the parent — treat as fully blocking.
The ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the child during its constraint scan applies in all versions.

title: ATTACH PARTITION with a pre-validated matching CHECK constraint (scan skipped — the safe pattern)
sqlPattern:
-- prep (non-blocking): ADD CONSTRAINT ... CHECK (...) NOT VALID; then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT;
ALTER TABLE measurement ATTACH PARTITION measurement_y2026m07 FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-07-01') TO ('2026-08-01');
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the partitioned parent (PG 12+); ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the child is taken but is momentary because no validation scan is needed; SHARE on FK-referencing tables; ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the DEFAULT partition only if one exists.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12+ AND the child carries a VALID CHECK constraint that exactly implies the target partition bounds (plus NOT NULL on the key for NULL-rejecting list partitions), so the planner-proven constraint lets Postgres skip the full scan.
No DEFAULT partition exists, or the DEFAULT partition also has a CHECK that excludes the new bounds so it too skips its scan.
unsafeWhen: The CHECK does not exactly match/imply the partition constraint — Postgres falls back to a full validation scan (degrades to the SCAN entry).
A partition key is an expression and the partition rejects NULLs — the CHECK-skip optimization does not apply and the scan runs.
PostgreSQL 11 or earlier — parent lock is ACCESS EXCLUSIVE regardless of the CHECK.
safeRewrite: This IS the safe rewrite. Steps: SET lock_timeout='2s'; ADD CONSTRAINT ... CHECK(...) NOT VALID (metadata-only, brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on child); VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (scan under SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, does not block DML); then ATTACH PARTITION (metadata-only). Retry the ATTACH with backoff on lock_timeout.
edgeCases: Build the CHECK as NOT VALID first, then VALIDATE separately so the scan happens under SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE rather than during a blocking ADD.
The redundant CHECK can be dropped after a successful ATTACH; PG keeps it harmless but it duplicates the implicit partition constraint.
If the child is itself partitioned, an appropriate CHECK also avoids locking all sub-partitions (docs 5.12.2.2).
versionNotes: CHECK-based scan skipping documented for ATTACH PARTITION across PG 11+.
SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE parent lock requires PG 12+.

title: ATTACH PARTITION when a DEFAULT partition exists (extra default scan)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE events ATTACH PARTITION events_q3 FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-07-01') TO ('2026-10-01'); -- events_default present
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the parent (PG 12+), ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the child being attached, and ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the DEFAULT partition while it is scanned to verify no existing rows fall into the newly claimed bounds.
safeWhen: The DEFAULT partition is empty or tiny, so its ACCESS EXCLUSIVE scan window is negligible.
The DEFAULT partition carries a CHECK constraint proving it holds no rows in the new bounds, letting Postgres skip the default scan.
unsafeWhen: The DEFAULT partition is large and hot: it is scanned under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, blocking all reads and writes to the default partition (where catch-all traffic often lands) for a duration that scales with its row count.
The default scan queues behind any long-running query touching the default partition, cascading a lock-queue pile-up on it.
safeRewrite: Give the DEFAULT partition a CHECK constraint that excludes the bounds of the partition you are about to attach (ADD ... NOT VALID then VALIDATE under SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE), so ATTACH skips the default scan. Better: avoid a DEFAULT partition in high-write designs specifically because every new ATTACH/CREATE must re-scan it. Keep lock_timeout short and retry.
edgeCases: This blocking is on the DEFAULT partition specifically (ACCESS EXCLUSIVE), not the parent — but the default partition is frequently the busiest table, so treat as read/write blocking.
The same default-partition scan is triggered by CREATE TABLE ... PARTITION OF when a default exists.
If the default partition is itself partitioned, locks propagate to its sub-partitions.
versionNotes: DEFAULT partitions introduced in PostgreSQL 11; the constraint-narrowing scan of the default applies from PG 11 onward.

title: DETACH PARTITION (normal, non-concurrent)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE measurement DETACH PARTITION measurement_y2026m07;
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on BOTH the partitioned parent and the partition being detached; SHARE on any tables that reference the parent via foreign-key constraints.
safeWhen: You are in a maintenance window and can tolerate the whole hierarchy being unavailable (reads and writes to ALL partitions block) for the brief lock window.
You explicitly need transactional/atomic detach semantics and cannot use CONCURRENTLY (e.g. inside a transaction block, or when a DEFAULT partition exists).
unsafeWhen: On a live OLTP hierarchy: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the parent blocks reads AND writes to every partition, and any long-running query makes the DETACH queue and stampede all following queries behind it.
PostgreSQL 14+ is available and you have no default partition / no txn-block requirement — in that case CONCURRENTLY is strictly better.
safeRewrite: On PG 14+ use DETACH PARTITION CONCURRENTLY (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the parent throughout, only a brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the partition at the very end). Must run outside a transaction block and requires no DEFAULT partition. Always set a short lock_timeout and retry. See 'detach-partition-concurrently'.
edgeCases: The operation itself is catalog-only (fast); the danger is purely the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE acquisition and the lock-queue pile-up, not scan/rewrite cost.
Detached indexes: indexes that were attached to the parent's partitioned indexes are detached and remain as standalone indexes on the now-standalone table.
Cloned triggers created from the parent's triggers are removed from the detached table.
Dependent views/FKs on the parent are unaffected structurally, but the SHARE lock on FK-referencing tables can itself contend.
versionNotes: All supported versions: normal DETACH takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on parent and partition.
Pre-PG 14 this was the ONLY option — historically the most dangerous partition operation on live systems.

title: DETACH PARTITION CONCURRENTLY (PG 14+)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE measurement DETACH PARTITION measurement_y2026m07 CONCURRENTLY;
lockMode: Two transactions. Txn 1: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on both parent and partition (marks partition as detach-pending), then commits and waits out concurrent transactions using the parent. Txn 2: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the parent and a brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the partition to finalize. A duplicate CHECK constraint mirroring the partition constraint is added to the detached table.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 14+, run OUTSIDE a transaction block, and the partitioned table has NO DEFAULT partition.
You can tolerate a very brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE window on the single partition being detached at the end (parent and other partitions stay readable/writable throughout).
unsafeWhen: The partitioned table has a DEFAULT partition — CONCURRENTLY is not allowed (the default would need an exclusive lock to re-widen its constraint). Falls back to normal DETACH.
You need it inside a transaction block (e.g. framework migration wrapping DDL in a txn) — CONCURRENTLY cannot run in a transaction block.
PostgreSQL 13 or earlier — option does not exist.
safeRewrite: This is the safe form. Ensure autocommit (no BEGIN/COMMIT wrapper), remove/avoid the DEFAULT partition first, set a short lock_timeout, and be prepared to run ALTER TABLE ... DETACH PARTITION ... FINALIZE if the command is interrupted (see 'detach-partition-finalize').
edgeCases: If txn 1 succeeds but the command is canceled/crashes, the partition is left in a 'detach pending' state; you must run DETACH ... FINALIZE (or re-run CONCURRENTLY) to complete it.
At most ONE partition per partitioned table may be in pending-detach state at a time.
The brief final ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the partition can still momentarily queue behind a long query on that specific partition — keep lock_timeout short.
A duplicate CHECK constraint equal to the old partition constraint is left on the detached standalone table (drop it if unwanted).
versionNotes: DETACH PARTITION CONCURRENTLY and DETACH PARTITION FINALIZE introduced in PostgreSQL 14.
Not usable when a DEFAULT partition exists (documented restriction), in any version.

title: DETACH PARTITION FINALIZE (complete an interrupted concurrent detach, PG 14+)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE measurement DETACH PARTITION measurement_y2026m07 FINALIZE;
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the parent and a brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the partition to complete the pending detach (same final-stage locking as txn 2 of CONCURRENTLY).
safeWhen: A previous DETACH ... CONCURRENTLY was canceled or interrupted, leaving the partition in detach-pending state, and you need to finish it.
PostgreSQL 14+.
unsafeWhen: No detach is actually pending (nothing to finalize).
The brief final ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the partition queues behind a long-running query on that partition.
safeRewrite: Run FINALIZE (or simply re-issue DETACH ... CONCURRENTLY) to resolve a stuck pending-detach; keep lock_timeout short. Detect the pending state via pg_class/pg_inherits or a failed re-detach error before running.
edgeCases: Only meaningful after an interrupted CONCURRENTLY detach; at most one pending detach exists per partitioned table.
Leaves the standalone table with its duplicate CHECK constraint just like a completed CONCURRENTLY detach.
versionNotes: Introduced in PostgreSQL 14 alongside DETACH ... CONCURRENTLY.

title: CREATE INDEX on a partitioned parent (recursing, non-concurrent)
sqlPattern: CREATE INDEX idx_measurement_ts ON measurement (logtime);
lockMode: SHARE on the partitioned parent and, recursively, on each partition while its index is built (standard CREATE INDEX takes SHARE, which locks out writes but not reads). The parent-level catalog change takes the strong table lock; each partition build blocks writes to that partition.
safeWhen: Small tables / maintenance window: you can accept writes being locked out on each partition while its index builds.
You need a single atomic statement and cannot orchestrate the per-partition concurrent workflow.
unsafeWhen: Live high-write hierarchy: SHARE lock blocks INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE on every partition as the build recurses; dwell time scales with total rows across all partitions.
Concurrent index builds on partitioned tables are NOT supported as a single command — you cannot say CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY ... on the partitioned parent to cover all partitions atomically.
safeRewrite: Use the metadata-only attach workflow: (1) CREATE INDEX ON ONLY parent (creates an INVALID parent index, catalog-only); (2) for each partition CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, non-blocking to writes); (3) ALTER INDEX parent_idx ATTACH PARTITION child_idx for each — once all partitions have matching indexes the parent index is automatically marked VALID. Building the partitioned index this way is a metadata-only operation. See 'create-index-partitioned-concurrent-workflow'.
edgeCases: Default behavior recurses to all partitions to ensure matching indexes; use ONLY to suppress recursion (leaves an INVALID parent index — the basis of the safe workflow).
Concurrent builds of expression and partial indexes ARE supported on individual partitions, just not on the partitioned parent as one statement.
TOAST: a plain b-tree over normal columns does not rewrite the heap; no heap/TOAST rewrite occurs here.
versionNotes: Partitioned indexes (CREATE INDEX recursing to partitions, ALTER INDEX ... ATTACH PARTITION) introduced in PostgreSQL 11.
Before PG 11 there were no native partitioned indexes.

title: Partitioned index the safe way: CREATE INDEX ON ONLY + per-partition CONCURRENTLY + ALTER INDEX ATTACH PARTITION
lockMode: Step 1 (ON ONLY parent): SHARE on the parent to create catalog entry for an INVALID index — no scan. Step 2 (CONCURRENTLY per partition): SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on that partition, does not block writes. Step 3 (ALTER INDEX ATTACH PARTITION): brief lock to attach; when the last partition attaches, the parent index flips to VALID as a metadata-only operation.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 11+ (partitioned indexes exist).
You want to minimize the write-lockout window on a live partitioned hierarchy: each partition is indexed with CONCURRENTLY (writes continue), and the parent finalize is metadata-only.
unsafeWhen: A per-partition CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY fails midway (deadlock/unique violation): it leaves an INVALID index on that partition, and the parent index stays INVALID until every partition has a matching valid index attached.
You forget to ATTACH a partition's index: the parent index remains INVALID and is ignored for querying.
safeRewrite: This is the recommended safe workflow itself. Automate: after each CONCURRENTLY build, verify the partition index is VALID (check pg_index.indisvalid) before ATTACH; clean up any INVALID leftovers with DROP INDEX / REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY; only then attach so the parent goes VALID.
edgeCases: The parent index is marked INVALID by CREATE INDEX ... ON ONLY and only becomes VALID once ALL partitions acquire matching indexes and are attached — an unfinished workflow leaves a permanently-ignored parent index.
A failed CONCURRENTLY build leaves an INVALID partition index that still incurs write overhead; drop it and retry, or REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY.
ALTER INDEX ... ATTACH PARTITION matches an existing equivalent partition index if one already exists rather than forcing a rebuild.
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY cannot run inside a transaction block and does two table scans plus a wait for concurrent transactions to finish.
versionNotes: CREATE INDEX ... ON ONLY, INVALID partitioned parent index, and ALTER INDEX ... ATTACH PARTITION: PostgreSQL 11+.
CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on individual (non-partitioned) tables: available since PG 8.2; still cannot target the partitioned parent as one statement.
_correction: CREATE INDEX ON ONLY <parent> (non-concurrent) takes SHARE on the parent (blocks writes, allows reads) — NOT ACCESS EXCLUSIVE.

title: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on a single partition (leaf table)
sqlPattern: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_p1 ON measurement_p1 (logtime);
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on that partition — builds without taking locks that prevent concurrent INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE; performs two table scans and waits for all transactions that could modify the index to finish.
safeWhen: Always the preferred way to add an index to a live leaf partition: writes and reads continue during the build.
Run outside a transaction block (required for CONCURRENTLY).
unsafeWhen: On failure (deadlock, unique violation, or a lock_timeout interruption) it leaves behind an INVALID index that is ignored for queries but still adds write overhead until cleaned up.
Cannot be used inside a transaction block — framework migrations that wrap DDL in a transaction will error.
safeRewrite: This is already the safe primitive. Wrap with cleanup: on failure DROP INDEX idx_p1; and retry, or REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx_p1; strong_migrations can auto-remove invalid indexes (StrongMigrations.remove_invalid_indexes = true). Set a short lock_timeout on the brief final catalog step and retry.
edgeCases: The psql \d command reports a failed leftover index as INVALID; recommended recovery is DROP + retry, or REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY.
Two full scans of the partition mean dwell scales with the partition's rows even though writes are not blocked.
Concurrent builds of expression and partial indexes are supported on a single partition.
versionNotes: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY available since PostgreSQL 8.2; REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY since PostgreSQL 12.
strong_migrations remove_invalid_indexes helper cleans leftover INVALID indexes automatically.

title: Lock-queue stampede: any parent-level ACCESS EXCLUSIVE that blocks becomes a hierarchy-wide outage
sqlPattern:
-- e.g. DETACH PARTITION (normal), CREATE TABLE ... PARTITION OF, PG<12 ATTACH PARTITION
SET lock_timeout = '2s'; ALTER TABLE parent DETACH PARTITION p1; -- retry on timeout
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE conflicts with locks of ALL modes (down to ACCESS SHARE held by plain SELECT). When it cannot be granted immediately it waits at the head of the lock queue, and every conflicting request queued behind it — including reads — waits too.
safeWhen: The conflicting partition DDL runs under a short lock_timeout (values under ~2s are common) with automatic retry/backoff, so a blocked statement aborts instead of freezing the hierarchy.
You have first eliminated long-running transactions/queries (monitor via log_lock_waits and log_min_duration_statement) that would make the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE wait.
unsafeWhen: A parent-level ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (normal DETACH, CREATE TABLE PARTITION OF, or ATTACH on PG<12) is issued while a long query holds even an ACCESS SHARE lock: the DDL queues, and subsequent SELECT/INSERT on the whole hierarchy pile up behind it — the migration is 'fast' but the wait causes the outage.
No lock_timeout is set: the pile-up persists until the long query finishes.
safeRewrite: Always SET lock_timeout in migration sessions (GoCardless/pgroll: sub-2s with retries). Prefer the low-lock variants that only take SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the parent (PG 12+ ATTACH, PG 14+ DETACH CONCURRENTLY) — SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE does NOT conflict with ACCESS SHARE/ROW EXCLUSIVE, so reads and writes are never queued behind it (it only conflicts with other DDL/VACUUM). Retry the DDL with exponential backoff.
edgeCases: This is a property of the lock, not the operation: even an instantaneous catalog change stampedes if its ACCESS EXCLUSIVE cannot be granted immediately.

SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE still conflicts with itself, SHARE, SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE, EXCLUSIVE, ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — so a low-lock ATTACH/DETACH can still queue behind autovacuum or another DDL, delaying subsequent DDL (but never blocking plain DML).
lock_timeout aborting the DDL is strictly preferable to production read/write downtime.
versionNotes: Applies to all versions; the mitigation is choosing PG 12+ ATTACH and PG 14+ DETACH CONCURRENTLY to avoid parent ACCESS EXCLUSIVE entirely.

title: VACUUM FULL [table]
sqlPattern: VACUUM FULL my_table;
safeRewrite: Do not run on a live hot table. To reclaim bloat with minimal blocking use pg_repack or pg_squeeze (online rewrite via triggers + short final swap), or set up a new partition/table and migrate. If OS-level space reclamation is not needed, a plain VACUUM (autovacuum) reclaims reusable space without an exclusive lock. If VACUUM FULL is unavoidable, schedule a maintenance window, set lock_timeout, and expect dwell proportional to table+TOAST bytes.
safeWhen: Table is small or a maintenance window with the table fully offline is available
You genuinely need to return disk space to the OS (plain VACUUM only reuses space within the table)
unsafeWhen: Table receives concurrent reads or writes and any downtime is unacceptable
Large table/TOAST: dwell scales with total bytes rewritten, so ACCESS EXCLUSIVE is held for the entire rewrite
edgeCases: Rewrites the entire heap into a new disk file and rebuilds all indexes; requires free disk space up to a second full copy of the table plus indexes until the operation completes
TOAST tables are rewritten as part of the operation, adding to dwell time for wide/large-value rows
Blocks even SELECTs — ACCESS EXCLUSIVE conflicts with ACCESS SHARE
If it must wait for an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock behind a long transaction, it queues at the head of the lock queue and stalls all subsequent reads/writes behind it (see lock-queue edge case)
versionNotes: Behavior consistent across all currently supported versions (14–18); VACUUM FULL has rewritten the heap into a new file since 9.0

title: VACUUM (without FULL) — safe contrast baseline
sqlPattern: VACUUM my_table;  -- or let autovacuum handle it
safeRewrite: Already safe: runs concurrently with reads and writes. Prefer letting autovacuum manage this; run manual VACUUM (or VACUUM (ANALYZE)) when you need immediate stats/space reuse. Does NOT return space to the OS — only marks it reusable within the table.
safeWhen: Any time — SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE does not conflict with ACCESS SHARE (reads) or ROW EXCLUSIVE (writes)
unsafeWhen: Only one VACUUM / SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE operation may run on a given table at a time (two self-conflict)
edgeCases: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE self-conflicts, so it cannot run concurrently with another VACUUM, ANALYZE, CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, or REINDEX CONCURRENTLY on the same table
Does not shrink the file on disk; use VACUUM FULL or pg_repack for OS reclamation
versionNotes: Consistent across all supported versions

title: CLUSTER [table] USING [index]
sqlPattern: CLUSTER my_table USING my_index;
safeRewrite: There is no built-in online CLUSTER. Use pg_repack --order-by (or pg_squeeze), which reorders the heap online via triggers and a short final ACCESS EXCLUSIVE swap. Note clustering is a one-time physical reorder — new/updated rows are NOT kept in index order — so the recurring cost rarely justifies the outage. If truly needed, run in a maintenance window with lock_timeout set.
safeWhen: Small table, or a full maintenance window with the table offline
One-off physical reorganization where the table can tolerate being unavailable for the rewrite
unsafeWhen: Table serves concurrent reads or writes
Large table: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE held for the whole physical reorder (dwell scales with bytes)
edgeCases: Physically rewrites/reorders the entire table and rebuilds all its indexes
Disk space: index-scan method needs free space = table size + index sizes; the seq-scan+sort path can need up to ~2x the table size plus index sizes in temporary space
Clustering is not maintained — subsequent inserts/updates are not auto-clustered; run ANALYZE afterward so the planner has fresh stats
Cannot run inside a transaction block for partitioned tables, and the index argument is mandatory there
Bare CLUSTER; (no table) re-clusters every previously-clustered table the user owns, each under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
versionNotes: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE behavior consistent across all supported versions (14–18)

title: REINDEX INDEX [name] (non-concurrent)
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the specific index; SHARE-level lock on the parent table (locks out writes, not reads)
sqlPattern: REINDEX INDEX my_index;
safeRewrite: Use REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY my_index; (PostgreSQL 12+), which holds only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE and allows reads and writes throughout. On <12, drop/recreate via CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY for a replacement then DROP INDEX the old one.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL < 12 where CONCURRENTLY is unavailable AND a write-blocking window is acceptable
Table tolerates writes being blocked for the duration of the index rebuild
Reindexing a system catalog index (CONCURRENTLY is not supported there)
unsafeWhen: Table takes concurrent writes that cannot be blocked
Queries whose plans use the target index will block on the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE index lock even though the table permits reads
edgeCases: Docs: 'REINDEX locks out writes but not reads of the index's parent table. It also takes an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock on the specific index being processed, which will block reads that attempt to use that index.' The planner takes ACCESS SHARE on every index of the table, so REINDEX blocks virtually any query except cached plans that don't reference this index — the Explicit Locking page therefore classifies REINDEX under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
Can run inside a transaction block (unlike CONCURRENTLY)
On temp tables REINDEX is always effectively non-concurrent (no other session can access them)
versionNotes: Non-concurrent REINDEX exists in all versions
CONCURRENTLY alternative available only in PostgreSQL 12+

title: REINDEX TABLE [name] (non-concurrent)
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on each index; SHARE-level lock on the table (locks out writes, allows reads)
sqlPattern: REINDEX TABLE my_table;
safeRewrite: Use REINDEX TABLE CONCURRENTLY my_table; (PostgreSQL 12+). It rebuilds all of the table's indexes one at a time under SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE while permitting reads and writes.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL < 12 with a write-blocking maintenance window
System-catalog reindex (REINDEX SYSTEM/DATABASE cannot be CONCURRENTLY)
unsafeWhen: Live table receiving writes that cannot be paused
Rebuild spans many/large indexes, extending the write-blocking window
edgeCases: Rebuilds every index on the table; writes are blocked for the whole operation, and queries using any of the indexes may block
Can run inside a transaction block
Exclusion-constraint indexes are handled fine here (unlike CONCURRENTLY, which skips them)
versionNotes: Exists in all versions; CONCURRENTLY variant is 12+

title: REINDEX INDEX/TABLE ... CONCURRENTLY
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (session-level, on the indexes rebuilt and their tables)
sqlPattern: REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY my_index;  -- or REINDEX TABLE CONCURRENTLY my_table;
safeRewrite: This IS the safe form. Run outside any transaction block. Set lock_timeout so the brief metadata/catalog steps that must acquire locks fail fast rather than queue. After a failure, sweep for and DROP INDEX any leftover *_ccnew / *_ccold invalid indexes before retrying.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12+ and the target is a normal (non-catalog, non-exclusion-constraint) index
Statement is not wrapped in a transaction block
You can tolerate a longer total build time in exchange for staying online
unsafeWhen: Reindexing system catalogs — 'system catalogs cannot be reindexed concurrently' (use plain REINDEX)
Indexes backing exclusion constraints — cannot be done concurrently; they are silently skipped when reindexing a whole table/database concurrently
Inside a transaction block (not allowed), including implicit blocks
PostgreSQL < 12 (option does not exist)
edgeCases: Only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE is held, so it does not block reads or writes — but SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE self-conflicts, so only one concurrent build can run per table at a time, and it cannot overlap VACUUM/ANALYZE/CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY on that table

Failure leaves an INVALID transient index: '_ccnew' suffix (new build) or '_ccold' suffix (old index that couldn't be dropped); recovery is DROP INDEX then retry. Numeric suffixes (_ccnew1, _ccold2) appear on repeated failures
Leftover _ccnew/_ccold indexes still consume write/update overhead and disk until dropped — an audit tool should flag them

The build must wait for all transactions that hold snapshots predating each phase to finish; a single long-running transaction can stall progress indefinitely (does not block others, but stalls the build)
Temporary tables are always reindexed non-concurrently
On partitioned relations each partition is processed in its own transaction, so the command cannot be inside a transaction block
Uses roughly double the disk of the index while both old and new exist
versionNotes: REINDEX CONCURRENTLY was ADDED in PostgreSQL 12 (takes ShareUpdateExclusiveLock). Not available in 11 or earlier.

title: REINDEX SYSTEM / REINDEX DATABASE
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE per index (SHARE-level on each table); CONCURRENTLY not allowed for SYSTEM
sqlPattern: REINDEX DATABASE mydb;  -- or REINDEX SYSTEM mydb;
safeRewrite: For a whole database, prefer per-index REINDEX ... CONCURRENTLY driven by a script (e.g. reindexdb --concurrently, PG 12+) so user indexes stay online; system catalog indexes still require non-concurrent REINDEX and a window. Avoid a single blanket REINDEX DATABASE on a live system.
safeWhen: Maintenance window with writes paused
System catalog corruption recovery (must be non-concurrent)
unsafeWhen: Live multi-tenant database — serially write-locks table after table
Attempting CONCURRENTLY on REINDEX SYSTEM (unsupported)
edgeCases: reindexdb --concurrently (PG 12+) rebuilds user indexes concurrently but falls back to non-concurrent for catalogs and skips exclusion-constraint indexes
Very long total runtime across all indexes; each index's window is independent
versionNotes: reindexdb --concurrently added in PostgreSQL 12
PostgreSQL 14 replaced REINDEX DATABASE's implicit catalog coverage; use REINDEX SYSTEM explicitly for catalogs

title: CREATE INDEX (non-concurrent) — related maintenance rebuild path
sqlPattern: CREATE INDEX idx ON my_table (col);
safeRewrite: Use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx ON my_table (col); outside a transaction block. The SHARE lock of a plain CREATE INDEX blocks all writes for the full scan. (Exception: indexes on a table created in the same migration/transaction don't need CONCURRENTLY.)
safeWhen: Table is brand new / created in the same transaction (no concurrent writers)
Short write-blocking window acceptable
unsafeWhen: Existing hot table taking writes — 'adding an index non-concurrently blocks writes' (strong_migrations)
edgeCases: SHARE lock allows concurrent reads but conflicts with ROW EXCLUSIVE, so INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE block until the single-scan build finishes
Can run inside a transaction block (unlike CONCURRENTLY)
versionNotes: Consistent across all supported versions

title: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY
sqlPattern: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx ON my_table (col);
safeRewrite: This is the safe form. Run outside any transaction block (in Rails/strong_migrations use disable_ddl_transaction!). Set lock_timeout. Verify the index is valid afterward (pg_index.indisvalid); if the build failed, DROP INDEX the leftover INVALID index before retrying.
safeWhen: Adding an index to a live table without blocking reads or writes
Statement runs outside a transaction block
unsafeWhen: Inside a transaction block (not permitted)
Assuming success without checking validity — a failed build leaves an INVALID index that still adds write overhead
edgeCases: Two-scan build in separate transactions; must wait for pre-existing transactions to complete, so a long-running transaction stalls it
On failure leaves an INVALID index (ignored by the planner but still maintained on writes); recovery is DROP INDEX then retry, or REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY
SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE self-conflicts: cannot overlap VACUUM/ANALYZE/another CIC/REINDEX CONCURRENTLY on the same table
CONCURRENTLY directly on a partitioned parent is restricted — build per-partition, then create the parent index and ATTACH; a UNIQUE/PK created this way needs each partition indexed first
versionNotes: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY available since PostgreSQL 8.2
PostgreSQL 14 reduced the chance that CIC/RIC are blocked by other CIC/RIC operations' snapshots (performance, not lock-mode, change)

title: Cross-cutting edge case: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE ops and the lock queue
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (VACUUM FULL / CLUSTER / plain REINDEX) waiting behind a held lock
sqlPattern: -- e.g. VACUUM FULL / CLUSTER / REINDEX blocked by a long-running SELECT
safeRewrite: Always set a short lock_timeout (values under 2s are common) before any ACCESS EXCLUSIVE maintenance op so it fails fast instead of parking at the head of the lock queue. Retry with backoff. Kill or avoid long-running transactions during the window.
safeWhen: lock_timeout is set so the operation cannot indefinitely head the queue
unsafeWhen: No lock_timeout AND a long-running transaction holds even an ACCESS SHARE lock on the target table
edgeCases: A CONDITIONAL amplifier, not a standalone op: an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request that must wait queues AHEAD of all later requests, so even plain SELECTs (ACCESS SHARE) pile up behind it — the table is effectively down for reads and writes until the maintenance op acquires and releases its lock
This turns a 'fast' operation into an outage purely because of a pre-existing long transaction

Concurrent forms (CONCURRENTLY / plain VACUUM) only take SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE and do not head the queue this way, but they instead WAIT for old snapshots to clear — stalling their own progress without blocking others
versionNotes: lock_timeout GUC available since PostgreSQL 9.3

title: Set a low lock_timeout before every DDL statement
lockMode: none (session GUC; governs how long a lock request in the same session may wait)
sqlPattern: SET lock_timeout = '50ms'; -- or up to a few seconds; then run the DDL. Alternatively SET LOCAL inside a txn, or ALTER ROLE ... SET lock_timeout for the migration role.
safeRewrite: Always prefix production DDL with SET lock_timeout to a value the app can tolerate (50ms-100ms with retries; up to ~2s single-shot). If the lock cannot be acquired in that window the statement fails with 'canceling statement due to lock_timeout' instead of parking an ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request at the head of the table's lock queue and stalling all traffic.
safeWhen: Value is small relative to app latency budget (Braintree/GoCardless: hold exclusive locks <= ~2s; depesz/postgres.ai: 50ms with a retry loop)
Set per-session or per-role so it cannot be forgotten
Paired with a retry-with-backoff loop so a fast failure is automatically re-attempted
unsafeWhen: lock_timeout = 0 (the default: wait indefinitely) — a DDL that blocks on a long transaction becomes head-of-line and queues ALL subsequent reads/writes
Value larger than the app's request timeout — the queue pileup causes an effective outage before the DDL even times out
edgeCases: lock_timeout only bounds the WAIT to acquire a lock, not the runtime of the operation once acquired — a long REWRITE/SCAN still runs to completion under statement_timeout
A statement that must take several locks (e.g. FK touching two tables) can be cancelled on any one of them
SET lock_timeout requires PostgreSQL 9.3+
versionNotes: lock_timeout GUC introduced in PostgreSQL 9.3; before that you had to emulate it with statement_timeout around an explicit LOCK TABLE

title: Head-of-line blocking: a waiting ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request queues everything behind it
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (the pending request that causes the pileup)
sqlPattern:
-- txn A: SELECT ..., pg_sleep(30) FROM users;  (holds ACCESS SHARE)
-- txn B: ALTER TABLE users ADD COLUMN age int;  (waits for ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, enters queue head)
-- txn C: SELECT * FROM users;  (ACCESS SHARE — now blocked behind B, even though it would not conflict with A)
safeRewrite: Because a queued lock request blocks every later request that conflicts with it, an ALTER TABLE waiting on ACCESS EXCLUSIVE locks out even plain SELECTs. Fix by bounding the wait with lock_timeout so B fails fast and drains the queue, then retrying — never let DDL wait indefinitely on a busy table.
safeWhen: DDL runs with a low lock_timeout so it cannot sit at the queue head
No long-running transactions hold a conflicting lock at the moment of the attempt (check pg_stat_activity / pg_locks first)
unsafeWhen: A long-running SELECT, idle-in-transaction session, autovacuum (to prevent wraparound), or open replica feedback holds any conflicting lock while DDL requests ACCESS EXCLUSIVE with no timeout
pg_dump is running (it holds ACCESS SHARE for the whole dump)
edgeCases: The DDL statement itself may be sub-millisecond once it gets the lock — the outage is entirely the WAIT window, which is why timeouts matter more than the operation's own cost
autovacuum's ACCESS EXCLUSIVE truncate phase can itself be the blocker
A transaction never conflicts with itself: it can already hold ACCESS SHARE and later escalate on the same table

title: Retry the DDL with (exponential, jittered) backoff after a lock_timeout failure
sqlPattern:
-- depesz shell loop:
while true; do psql -qX -v ON_ERROR_STOP=1 -f alter.sql && break; sleep 1; done
-- postgres.ai backoff: delay_ms = round(random() * least(cap_ms, base_ms * 2 ^ attempt)); base_ms=10, cap_ms=60000
safeRewrite: Wrap the timeout-guarded DDL in a retry loop: catch lock_not_available, sleep with exponential backoff + jitter, and re-attempt. With lock_timeout='50ms' and ~1000 attempts you get ~17 minutes of opportunistic tries; the DDL slips in the moment all transactions that started BEFORE a given attempt finish (you never have to wait for future transactions, since they queue behind your pending request during that attempt).
safeWhen: Each attempt uses a fresh, short lock_timeout
Backoff caps and total attempt budget are bounded so a truly stuck table eventually fails the deploy loudly
Retries happen OUTSIDE a long-lived transaction
unsafeWhen: Retrying via SAVEPOINT/subtransaction inside one long transaction — each rollback burns XIDs and can overflow the subtransaction SLRU on replicas, causing drastic slowdowns
No backoff (tight loop) — adds load and starves the very transactions you need to finish
edgeCases: Multiple DDLs batched in a single transaction with subtransaction retries hold every acquired lock until COMMIT and accumulate bloat via a long-held snapshot
Monitor pg_stat_slru (PG13+) when using subtransaction-based retries
Fixed 1s sleep is fine for hand-run migrations; automated tools (pgroll) prefer exponential backoff to reduce contention
versionNotes: pg_stat_slru available PostgreSQL 13+ for observing subtransaction SLRU pressure
strong_migrations exposes lock_timeout_retries / lock_timeout_retry_delay (experimental)

title: statement_timeout to bound total DDL runtime (and its interaction with lock_timeout)
lockMode: none (session GUC bounding total statement runtime)
safeRewrite: Use lock_timeout to bound the WAIT for a lock and statement_timeout to bound total RUNTIME (scan/rewrite). strong_migrations' defaults are lock_timeout=10s, statement_timeout=1h. Set statement_timeout high enough that a legitimate scan/rewrite completes but low enough that a runaway operation is killed; never rely on statement_timeout alone to protect the lock queue.
safeWhen: lock_timeout is small (protects the queue) and statement_timeout is sized to the expected scan/rewrite duration
For CONCURRENTLY operations, statement_timeout is generous — they legitimately run long
unsafeWhen: Using only statement_timeout as the lock guard: a large statement_timeout means DDL can still sit at the queue head for that whole window
statement_timeout set so low it kills a legitimate CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY / VALIDATE CONSTRAINT mid-scan
edgeCases: statement_timeout aborts CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY mid-build, leaving an INVALID index that must be dropped
In the explicit-LOCK pattern, set statement_timeout back to 0 only AFTER the lock is held, so the unbounded operation runs but the wait was bounded
Since 9.3, lock_timeout is preferred because it affects only lock-acquisition, not the operation
versionNotes: lock_timeout added 9.3; before that the LOCK TABLE + statement_timeout dance was the only fast-fail option

title: ADD COLUMN, nullable, no DEFAULT
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c varchar(30);
safeRewrite: Safe as-is under the protocol: it only updates the catalog (existing rows read NULL), so the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock is momentary. Still guard with lock_timeout + retry, because even a momentary ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request blocks the whole table if it has to wait behind a long transaction.
safeWhen: No DEFAULT and no NOT NULL
Wrapped in lock_timeout + retry
unsafeWhen: Run with lock_timeout=0 on a busy table with long-running transactions (queue pileup)
edgeCases: ORM/prepared-statement caches (e.g. ActiveRecord) may need a reconnect to see the new column; deploy code that tolerates the column's presence/absence

title: ADD COLUMN with a constant / non-volatile DEFAULT
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int DEFAULT 42;  -- or DEFAULT now() (now() is stable, evaluated once)
safeRewrite: On PostgreSQL 11+, a non-volatile default is stored once as a 'fast default' in the catalog and existing rows are NOT rewritten — METADATA_ONLY, safe under the protocol. On PostgreSQL <= 10 this REWRITES the whole table (blocks reads+writes for the rewrite); there, split into: ADD COLUMN (no default) -> SET DEFAULT -> batched backfill.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 11+ AND the default expression is non-volatile (constant, or a stable/immutable function evaluated once such as now())
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL 10 or earlier (any default triggers a full rewrite)
Default is a volatile function (see separate entry)
edgeCases: CONDITIONAL = METADATA_ONLY on 11+ with a non-volatile default, REWRITE otherwise
now()/CURRENT_TIMESTAMP are STABLE and captured once, so every existing row gets the same timestamp — that is intended fast-default behavior, not per-row evaluation
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 11: ADD COLUMN with a non-volatile DEFAULT no longer rewrites the table (fast default)

title: ADD COLUMN with a VOLATILE DEFAULT
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c timestamptz DEFAULT clock_timestamp();  -- or gen_random_uuid()
safeRewrite: A volatile default must be evaluated per row, forcing a full heap rewrite that holds ACCESS EXCLUSIVE for the whole rewrite (dwell scales with table bytes) — never safe in one shot. Rewrite as: (1) ADD COLUMN c timestamptz; (2) ALTER COLUMN c SET DEFAULT clock_timestamp() (affects only new rows); (3) backfill existing rows in throttled batches outside any long transaction.
safeWhen: Only after decomposing into add-nullable + set-default + batched backfill
unsafeWhen: Run as a single ALTER TABLE ADD COLUMN ... DEFAULT <volatile>() on any version — the fast-default optimization does NOT apply to volatile defaults, so it always rewrites
edgeCases: Even on PostgreSQL 11+ the fast-default path is bypassed for volatile expressions
gen_random_uuid(), random(), clock_timestamp(), nextval() are volatile
The rewrite doubles disk usage transiently and rebuilds all indexes
versionNotes: Fast-default optimization (PG11+) explicitly does not cover volatile defaults

title: ADD COLUMN ... NOT NULL DEFAULT <const>
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN c int NOT NULL DEFAULT 0;
