safeRewrite: On PostgreSQL 11+ with a non-volatile default this is METADATA_ONLY: the fast default satisfies NOT NULL for all existing rows without a scan or rewrite — safe under the protocol. On <=10 it REWRITES the table. If the default is volatile, or you want no default on new rows, use the multi-step: add nullable column -> backfill -> add CHECK (c IS NOT NULL) NOT VALID -> VALIDATE -> SET NOT NULL.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 11+ AND non-volatile default value
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL <= 10 (full rewrite while holding ACCESS EXCLUSIVE)
Volatile default (rewrite regardless of version)
edgeCases: CONDITIONAL: METADATA_ONLY on 11+/non-volatile, REWRITE otherwise
NOT NULL with NO default on a pre-populated column still needs the SET NOT NULL scan path (see set-not-null entry)
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 11 fast default makes NOT NULL + constant DEFAULT a metadata-only add

title: ALTER COLUMN ... SET DATA TYPE
sqlPattern:
ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c TYPE varchar(80);  -- rewrite
-- No-rewrite example (binary coercible): varchar(20) -> varchar(40), or -> text
safeRewrite: Normally rewrites the whole table and all its indexes under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (blocks reads+writes; dwell scales with bytes) — unsafe in one shot. Safe pattern: add a new column of the target type, dual-write from the app, backfill in batches, swap reads, then drop the old column. Exception: if the old type is binary-coercible to the new (e.g. varchar(n) widening, varchar->text) and the USING clause doesn't change contents, no heap rewrite occurs (though indexes may still be rebuilt).
safeWhen: The change is binary-coercible with no content change (widening varchar, varchar<->text, numeric with looser precision in some cases) — no rewrite
Otherwise only via the add-new-column / dual-write / backfill / swap dance
unsafeWhen: Any type change requiring per-row conversion (int->bigint (always a full REWRITE, all versions), timestamp<->timestamptz with tz math, adding/tightening precision) — full rewrite
edgeCases: Even binary-coercible changes can force index rebuilds unless the planner proves logical equivalence
Changing a type referenced by a foreign key re-checks the FK
Dependent views/functions may need to be dropped and recreated
_correction: int->bigint ALWAYS requires a full table REWRITE in every PostgreSQL version (4-byte vs 8-byte on-disk); there is no PG14 change making it metadata-only. (The REWRITE classification is correct.)

title: ALTER COLUMN ... SET NOT NULL on an existing column
safeRewrite: SET NOT NULL directly requires a full table SCAN while holding ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (blocks reads+writes for the scan). On PostgreSQL 12+, first add a validated CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) constraint (via NOT VALID + VALIDATE, which uses only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE and blocks nothing); SET NOT NULL then skips its scan and is METADATA_ONLY. The redundant CHECK can be dropped afterward.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12+ AND a matching validated CHECK (col IS NOT NULL) already exists — SET NOT NULL is then metadata-only
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL <= 11: SET NOT NULL always scans under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (no CHECK shortcut)
Running the bare SET NOT NULL on a large table without the CHECK precondition
edgeCases: CONDITIONAL: SCAN normally; METADATA_ONLY on 12+ when a valid IS NOT NULL CHECK proves it
The CHECK/VALIDATE approach moves the scan under SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, which does not block reads or writes but does conflict with other DDL/VACUUM
On partitioned tables SET NOT NULL has extra restrictions per docs
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 12: SET NOT NULL skips its validating scan if an existing valid CHECK constraint proves no NULLs

title: ALTER COLUMN ... DROP NOT NULL
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c DROP NOT NULL;
safeRewrite: Metadata-only: no scan, no rewrite. Safe under the protocol (brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE) — just guard with lock_timeout + retry.
safeWhen: Non-partitioned table (or a partition-consistent change)
unsafeWhen: Only the usual queue risk if run with lock_timeout=0
edgeCases: Restrictions apply on partitioned tables: you cannot drop NOT NULL on a partition column if the parent still requires it

title: ADD CONSTRAINT ... CHECK (...) NOT VALID, then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (brief, for NOT VALID add) then SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (for VALIDATE)
sqlPattern:
ALTER TABLE t ADD CONSTRAINT ck CHECK (c > 0) NOT VALID;  -- brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, no scan
ALTER TABLE t VALIDATE CONSTRAINT ck;                       -- SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, scans, no read/write block
safeRewrite: Adding a CHECK constraint in one step takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and SCANS the table to validate (blocks reads+writes for the whole scan) — unsafe. Split it: ADD ... NOT VALID (brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE, catalog-only, immediately enforced for NEW rows), then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE — scans existing rows but blocks neither reads nor writes). The scan cost is unchanged; the lock it runs under is drastically weaker.
safeWhen: Always prefer NOT VALID + separate VALIDATE for large tables
unsafeWhen: Single-statement ADD CONSTRAINT CHECK (without NOT VALID) on a large table — scans under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
edgeCases: NOT VALID means existing rows are NOT checked, but the constraint IS enforced for all new inserts/updates immediately
VALIDATE takes SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE which conflicts with other DDL, VACUUM, and ANALYZE on the table — serialize migration steps
A never-validated (permanently NOT VALID) CHECK will not be usable by the planner for constraint exclusion / SET NOT NULL shortcut
versionNotes: CHECK ... NOT VALID + VALIDATE available since PostgreSQL 9.2/9.4-era; VALIDATE's SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE behavior is the modern norm

title: ADD FOREIGN KEY ... NOT VALID, then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT
lockMode: SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE on both tables (brief for NOT VALID) then SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (for VALIDATE)
sqlPattern:
ALTER TABLE child ADD CONSTRAINT fk FOREIGN KEY (pid) REFERENCES parent(id) NOT VALID;  -- brief
ALTER TABLE child VALIDATE CONSTRAINT fk;  -- SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, scans child, no read/write block
safeRewrite: A single-statement ADD FOREIGN KEY takes SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE on BOTH child and parent and scans the child to validate existing rows — that lock conflicts with writes (ROW EXCLUSIVE) on both tables for the scan's duration. Split it: ADD ... NOT VALID (enforced for new rows immediately, brief lock), then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, blocks neither reads nor writes).
safeWhen: Use NOT VALID + separate VALIDATE, each guarded by lock_timeout + retry, especially with a hot parent table
unsafeWhen: Single-statement ADD FOREIGN KEY on large/busy tables — blocks writes on both tables during the validating scan
edgeCases: SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE does NOT block reads (ACCESS SHARE) but DOES block INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE and other DDL
The lock is taken on the REFERENCED table too — a migration on a small child can stall writes on a hot parent
FK add takes SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE rather than ACCESS EXCLUSIVE since PostgreSQL 9.5
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 9.5: ADD FOREIGN KEY reduced from ACCESS EXCLUSIVE to SHARE ROW EXCLUSIVE

title: Add UNIQUE / PRIMARY KEY without blocking (concurrent index + ADD CONSTRAINT USING INDEX)
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (index build) then ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (brief, ADD CONSTRAINT USING INDEX)
safeRewrite: ADD CONSTRAINT UNIQUE / PRIMARY KEY directly builds the backing index under a blocking SHARE lock (blocks writes) or ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and scans the whole table. Instead build the unique index with CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (blocks nothing), then attach it with ALTER TABLE ... ADD CONSTRAINT ... USING INDEX, which is a brief metadata-only catalog operation.
safeWhen: Index built CONCURRENTLY first, then attached via USING INDEX
For PRIMARY KEY, the column is already NOT NULL (established via the safe SET NOT NULL path)
unsafeWhen: ALTER TABLE ADD CONSTRAINT ... UNIQUE/PRIMARY KEY in one statement (builds index under blocking lock and scans)
edgeCases: If CREATE UNIQUE INDEX CONCURRENTLY fails on a duplicate it leaves an INVALID index that still enforces uniqueness — drop it before retrying
PRIMARY KEY additionally requires the column be NOT NULL, which itself needs the safe SET NOT NULL sequence
The index name becomes the constraint name after USING INDEX

title: CREATE INDEX (non-concurrent)
sqlPattern: CREATE INDEX idx ON t (c);
safeRewrite: Non-concurrent CREATE INDEX takes a SHARE lock and builds in a single scan: reads continue, but INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE block for the entire build (dwell scales with rows). On any non-trivial table use CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY instead.
safeWhen: Table is tiny / not yet in production traffic
Inside a transaction where CONCURRENTLY is not allowed AND the table is trivially small
unsafeWhen: Any busy table — writes are locked out for the whole build
edgeCases: SHARE conflicts with ROW EXCLUSIVE (writes) and with SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (VACUUM/ANALYZE) but not with ACCESS SHARE (reads)
Unlike CONCURRENTLY, a failed non-concurrent build rolls back cleanly (no leftover INVALID index)

title: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY (CIC)
sqlPattern: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx ON t (c);  -- cannot run inside a transaction block
safeRewrite: The safe default for adding indexes: takes only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, does two table scans and waits out concurrent transactions, and blocks neither reads nor writes (dwell scales with rows but is off the critical path). Cannot run inside an explicit transaction. Give it a generous statement_timeout; keep lock_timeout low only for the brief metadata steps.
safeWhen: Run outside a transaction block, with monitoring for leftover INVALID indexes
One concurrent build per table at a time
unsafeWhen: Inside a BEGIN/COMMIT block (not allowed) or via a tool that wraps DDL in a transaction (Rails needs disable_ddl_transaction!)
On a partitioned parent table directly (not supported — build per-partition, then attach)
edgeCases: A failed/cancelled CIC leaves an INVALID index (pg_index.indisvalid = false) that consumes write overhead but is ignored by the planner — detect with a pg_index query and DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY it, then retry
A UNIQUE CIC that fails on a duplicate still enforces uniqueness while INVALID
Long-running transactions and open snapshots delay CIC completion because it must wait them out
Before PostgreSQL 14, CIC held a snapshot that could block autovacuum (xmin horizon) system-wide
versionNotes: PostgreSQL 14: CIC no longer holds back the global xmin horizon the way it did previously, reducing autovacuum interference
Concurrent build on partitioned tables still unsupported — build each partition's index CONCURRENTLY, then CREATE INDEX (non-concurrent, metadata-only) on the parent to attach

title: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY
sqlPattern: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx;
safeRewrite: Plain DROP INDEX takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (blocks reads+writes briefly). Use DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY, which takes only SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE and blocks nothing. Cannot run inside a transaction block; cannot drop a constraint-backing index (PK/UNIQUE) — drop the constraint instead.
safeWhen: Standalone index, run outside a transaction block
unsafeWhen: Index backs a PRIMARY KEY or UNIQUE constraint (must ALTER TABLE DROP CONSTRAINT, which takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE)
Inside a transaction block (not allowed)
edgeCases: Only one CONCURRENTLY index operation per session at a time
Removing an index the planner relies on can regress query plans — verify first
versionNotes: DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY added in PostgreSQL 9.2

title: REINDEX INDEX/TABLE CONCURRENTLY
sqlPattern: REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx;  -- or REINDEX TABLE CONCURRENTLY t;
safeRewrite: Rebuild a bloated/corrupt index without blocking: REINDEX CONCURRENTLY takes SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, builds a replacement and swaps it in, blocking neither reads nor writes (dwell scales with index size). Also the recommended recovery for an INVALID index left by a failed CIC. On PostgreSQL <12, emulate with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY new -> DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY old -> rename.
safeWhen: PostgreSQL 12+, run outside a transaction block
unsafeWhen: PostgreSQL <= 11 (no REINDEX CONCURRENTLY — use the create/drop/rename dance)
Plain REINDEX (non-concurrent) takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and blocks reads+writes
edgeCases: A failed REINDEX CONCURRENTLY can leave an extra INVALID index (often suffixed _ccnew) that must be dropped
REINDEX TABLE CONCURRENTLY rebuilds all of a table's indexes
versionNotes: REINDEX CONCURRENTLY added in PostgreSQL 12
_correction: Plain (non-concurrent) REINDEX blocks WRITES but not table READS: it takes a SHARE-style lock on the table and ACCESS EXCLUSIVE only on the specific index being rebuilt. It does NOT block table reads.

title: DROP COLUMN
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP COLUMN c;
safeRewrite: Metadata-only: the column is marked dropped in the catalog, not physically removed, so the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock is brief. Safe under the protocol (lock_timeout + retry). The real risk is application-side: cached column lists break until app restart/reconnect, so deploy code that ignores the column first.
safeWhen: Application no longer references the column (e.g. ActiveRecord ignored_columns deployed first)
unsafeWhen: App still SELECTs the column or caches its schema (errors until reboot)
Column is referenced by a view, index, or FK (drop those dependencies first, or use CASCADE knowingly)
edgeCases: Space is not reclaimed immediately — dead column data lingers until rows are rewritten (UPDATE/VACUUM FULL/pg_repack)
DROP COLUMN ... CASCADE will silently drop dependent views/constraints
Dropping a column does not rewrite the table, so TOASTed values for it remain until rows are updated

title: RENAME COLUMN / RENAME TABLE
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t RENAME COLUMN a TO b;  -- or ALTER TABLE t RENAME TO t2;
safeRewrite: Cheap at the database level (catalog-only, brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE) but dangerous for zero-downtime because running app code references the old name and breaks instantly. Safe path: add the new column/table, dual-write, backfill, migrate reads, then drop the old — a multi-deploy expand/contract, not a rename.
safeWhen: No application depends on the old name during the switch (rare in live systems)
unsafeWhen: Any deployed code still references the old name — instant errors on the currently-running version
edgeCases: Views, functions, FKs, and default expressions referencing the old name follow the rename automatically (they store OIDs), but application SQL and ORMs do not
Renaming a table breaks prepared statements and cached plans in existing sessions

title: ALTER COLUMN SET DEFAULT / DROP DEFAULT
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c SET DEFAULT 'x';  -- or DROP DEFAULT
safeRewrite: Only changes the catalog default for FUTURE inserts/updates; existing rows are untouched, so no scan or rewrite. Safe under the protocol with a brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock — guard with lock_timeout + retry.
safeWhen: Always safe at the storage level (metadata only)
unsafeWhen: Only the generic queue risk if run with lock_timeout=0
edgeCases: Changing a default does NOT backfill existing rows — a separate batched UPDATE is needed for that
This is the second step of the safe volatile-default add pattern

title: ADD COLUMN ... GENERATED ALWAYS AS (...) STORED vs VIRTUAL
sqlPattern:
ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN g int GENERATED ALWAYS AS (a + b) STORED;   -- REWRITE
ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN g int GENERATED ALWAYS AS (a + b) VIRTUAL;   -- metadata-only (PG18+)
safeRewrite: A STORED generated column must compute and persist a value for every existing row, forcing a full heap REWRITE under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (blocks reads+writes; dwell scales with bytes) — decompose or accept a maintenance window. A VIRTUAL generated column (PostgreSQL 18+) stores nothing and is METADATA_ONLY. Alternative to STORED: an ordinary column maintained by a trigger, backfilled in batches.
safeWhen: VIRTUAL generated column on PostgreSQL 18+ (never rewrites)
unsafeWhen: STORED generated column on any version (full rewrite)
edgeCases: CONDITIONAL: REWRITE for STORED, METADATA_ONLY for VIRTUAL
VIRTUAL columns compute on read and cannot be indexed the same way STORED can
The rewrite transiently needs up to ~2x the table's disk space and rebuilds indexes
versionNotes: Generated columns (STORED) added in PostgreSQL 12
VIRTUAL generated columns added in PostgreSQL 18 (no rewrite)

title: ALTER COLUMN SET STATISTICS / SET (n_distinct = ...)
sqlPattern:
ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c SET STATISTICS 1000;
ALTER TABLE t ALTER COLUMN c SET (n_distinct = 200);
safeRewrite: Planner-tuning knobs that only update the catalog and take the weak SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock — they block neither reads nor writes. Safe under the protocol. (Run ANALYZE afterward to actually recompute stats with the new target.)
safeWhen: Always safe at the storage level; only conflicts with other DDL/VACUUM
unsafeWhen: Concurrent with another SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE operation on the same table (they serialize)
edgeCases: SET STATISTICS changes the sample target but does not itself recompute statistics until the next ANALYZE/autovacuum
SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE conflicts with VACUUM, ANALYZE, CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY, and other ALTERs

title: ALTER TABLE ... DROP CONSTRAINT
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t DROP CONSTRAINT ck;
safeRewrite: Dropping a CHECK/FK/UNIQUE/PK constraint is catalog-only (brief ACCESS EXCLUSIVE) — no scan, no rewrite. Safe under the protocol with lock_timeout + retry. Note: dropping a UNIQUE/PK constraint also drops its backing index (that drop is part of the same ACCESS EXCLUSIVE operation, not concurrent).
safeWhen: Constraint is no longer needed and nothing depends on it
unsafeWhen: Only the generic queue risk with lock_timeout=0
DROP CONSTRAINT ... CASCADE may drop dependent objects (e.g. FKs referencing a dropped PK)
edgeCases: Dropping a PRIMARY KEY/UNIQUE constraint removes its index under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE — you cannot make that index removal concurrent
Foreign keys in OTHER tables that reference this table's PK/UNIQUE will block the drop unless CASCADEd

title: ATTACH PARTITION / DETACH PARTITION [CONCURRENTLY]
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on parent (ATTACH, and DETACH CONCURRENTLY) / ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the partition being attached
sqlPattern:
ALTER TABLE parent ATTACH PARTITION p FOR VALUES FROM ('2026-01-01') TO ('2026-02-01');
ALTER TABLE parent DETACH PARTITION p CONCURRENTLY;
safeRewrite: ATTACH takes SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the parent (does not block parent reads/writes) but scans the incoming partition to prove its rows satisfy the bounds — add a matching validated CHECK constraint on the partition first so the scan is skipped. DETACH PARTITION CONCURRENTLY (PostgreSQL 14+) uses SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE and a two-phase approach to avoid blocking; plain DETACH takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the parent.
safeWhen: ATTACH: partition already has a validated CHECK matching the partition bound (scan skipped)
DETACH CONCURRENTLY on PostgreSQL 14+
unsafeWhen: Plain (non-CONCURRENTLY) DETACH takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the parent — blocks reads+writes briefly
ATTACH without a pre-validating CHECK scans the whole incoming partition
edgeCases: CONDITIONAL: METADATA_ONLY when a pre-existing CHECK lets ATTACH skip the scan, otherwise SCAN of the incoming partition
ATTACH also takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the partition being attached and on any default partition (whose rows are scanned)
DETACH CONCURRENTLY cannot run in a transaction block and can leave a partition in a transient DETACH-pending state if interrupted (finish with DETACH ... FINALIZE)
versionNotes: ATTACH PARTITION takes SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE on the parent (not ACCESS EXCLUSIVE) since PostgreSQL 12
DETACH PARTITION CONCURRENTLY added in PostgreSQL 14

title: Backfill existing rows in throttled batches (never in the DDL transaction)
lockMode: ROW EXCLUSIVE (ordinary write locks, per batch)
sqlPattern:
-- outside any DDL transaction, loop:
UPDATE t SET c = <val> WHERE id BETWEEN $lo AND $hi AND c IS NULL;  -- batches of ~1k-10k, sleep between
safeRewrite: Backfilling in the SAME transaction that runs the ALTER holds the table's lock for the entire backfill — never do that. Run the backfill separately, in bounded batches (e.g. 1k-10k rows) by primary-key range, committing each batch and sleeping between them to let autovacuum keep up and avoid replication lag. Only ordinary ROW EXCLUSIVE write locks are taken, so reads and other writes proceed.
safeWhen: Batched, each batch its own transaction, with a sleep/throttle between batches
Runs with disable_ddl_transaction! (or equivalent) so it is not wrapped in the migration's transaction
unsafeWhen: A single unbounded UPDATE of the whole table (bloats, long transaction, holds a snapshot that blocks vacuum, can lag replicas)
Backfill combined into the ALTER TABLE transaction (holds DDL locks for the whole backfill)
edgeCases: Each large UPDATE creates dead tuples — pace batches so autovacuum reclaims them and the table does not bloat
Long-running backfill transactions raise the xmin horizon and can stall CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY and VACUUM elsewhere
On primary/replica setups, unthrottled backfills generate WAL faster than replicas can apply

title: TRUNCATE / CLUSTER / VACUUM FULL / plain REINDEX / REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW (non-concurrent)
sqlPattern: TRUNCATE t;  CLUSTER t USING idx;  VACUUM FULL t;  REINDEX TABLE t;  REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW mv;
safeRewrite: These take ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and rewrite/lock the whole object, blocking reads AND writes for the full duration — they are maintenance operations, not zero-downtime migrations. Substitute the concurrent variants: pg_repack instead of CLUSTER/VACUUM FULL, REINDEX CONCURRENTLY instead of REINDEX, REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY (needs a unique index) instead of plain REFRESH. If unavoidable, schedule a maintenance window and still guard with lock_timeout to protect the queue.
safeWhen: Only in an explicit maintenance window, or via concurrent/online substitutes
unsafeWhen: Run against a live table during traffic — full read+write outage for the object
edgeCases: REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY takes EXCLUSIVE (allows reads, blocks writes to the MV) and requires a UNIQUE index on the MV; plain REFRESH takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
VACUUM FULL and CLUSTER need up to ~2x disk space during the rewrite
TRUNCATE is fast but still ACCESS EXCLUSIVE and cannot be batched
versionNotes: REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW CONCURRENTLY added in PostgreSQL 9.4
_correction: Exception: plain REINDEX does NOT belong in the ACCESS-EXCLUSIVE-blocks-reads group. It takes SHARE on the table (blocks writes, allows reads) + ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on the index only. TRUNCATE/CLUSTER/VACUUM FULL/REFRESH MATERIALIZED VIEW do block reads+writes.

title: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE DDL queued behind a long-running query stalls the whole table
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (requested by the DDL; not yet granted)
sqlPattern: ALTER TABLE t ADD COLUMN ...;  -- while a slow SELECT holds ACCESS SHARE on t
safeWhen: No transaction currently holds a lock on the table that conflicts with ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (i.e. the DDL can be granted the lock immediately)
A short lock_timeout is set so a failed acquisition releases the queue instead of parking it
The DDL itself, once granted, is catalog-only and releases in milliseconds
unsafeWhen: Any concurrent transaction holds a conflicting lock (even a plain SELECT's ACCESS SHARE) so the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request parks in the wait queue

Because a new lock request must wait if it conflicts with an already-waiting request, and ACCESS EXCLUSIVE conflicts with ALL modes, every subsequent statement — including plain SELECTs needing only ACCESS SHARE — queues behind the parked DDL
The table is effectively unavailable for reads and writes for the entire remaining duration of the original blocking query, not just the DDL's own runtime
safeRewrite: Set a short lock_timeout before the statement and wrap it in an application-side retry/backoff loop, e.g. `SET lock_timeout = '2s'; ALTER TABLE t ...;` (depesz uses 50ms; strong_migrations defaults to 10s). On SQLSTATE 55P03 (lock_not_available) the statement aborts immediately, releasing any partial hold so the queue drains; retry with exponential backoff. Additionally verify no long transactions are running (pg_stat_activity) before issuing the DDL.
edgeCases: idle-in-transaction sessions hold ACCESS SHARE indefinitely and are a common invisible blocker
pg_dump holds ACCESS SHARE on every dumped table for the entire dump duration
Regular autovacuum yields its SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE lock to a conflicting DDL, but anti-wraparound (aggressive) autovacuum does NOT yield and can block the DDL until it finishes
The blocking is FIFO among conflicting requests; non-conflicting requests behind a fully-conflicting AE request cannot 'jump' it
versionNotes: lock_timeout GUC available since PostgreSQL 9.3
idle_in_transaction_session_timeout (to auto-kill idle-in-txn blockers) available since 9.6

title: Bounded lock_timeout + retry loop — the safe lock-acquisition pattern
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (held only for the sub-timeout acquisition window, then briefly for the catalog change)
sqlPattern: SET lock_timeout = '2s'; ALTER TABLE t ...;  -- retry on 55P03 with backoff
safeWhen: lock_timeout is short enough that the maximum blocking window (queue-park time) is tolerable for the application — values under 2s are common, depesz uses 50ms
The underlying DDL is itself metadata-only (ADD nullable column, DROP CONSTRAINT, ATTACH validated constraint, rename) so once granted it releases in ~constant time
A retry loop exists so a timed-out attempt is re-issued rather than the migration failing
unsafeWhen: lock_timeout is long (or unset → infinite): the AE request parks the queue for up to that long, reintroducing the outage of entry lock-queue-ae-behind-long-read
The DDL wrapped in this pattern is actually a SCAN or REWRITE operation — the timeout bounds only the *wait*, not the hold; once granted, the AE lock is held for the whole scan/rewrite
safeRewrite: This IS the safe rewrite for metadata-only DDL. Pattern: per-session `SET lock_timeout` (never rely on server default), single-statement transaction, application retry with exponential backoff. For SCAN/REWRITE operations this pattern is necessary but NOT sufficient — the operation must first be decomposed into a concurrent/lock-free form (see rewrite-ddl-wal-flood-replica-lag and cic-waits-on-long-txn).
edgeCases: lock_timeout set inside the DDL transaction vs outside changes retry granularity: strong_migrations retries the whole migration if the timeout fires inside the DDL transaction, but only the statement if outside
SET LOCAL scopes the timeout to the current transaction; a bare SET persists for the session and can surprise later statements on a pooled connection
versionNotes: lock_timeout since 9.3; retries are an application concern (Postgres never auto-retries a lock acquisition)

title: lock_timeout vs statement_timeout must be tuned in opposition for migration sessions
lockMode: N/A (session GUCs governing lock wait and total statement runtime)
sqlPattern: SET lock_timeout = '10s'; SET statement_timeout = '1h';  -- migration role
safeWhen: lock_timeout is SHORT (fail fast on a parked queue) while statement_timeout is GENEROUS enough to let a legitimately long operation (backfill, non-concurrent index build) finish
Set on the migration role: ALTER ROLE migrator SET lock_timeout='10s'; ALTER ROLE migrator SET statement_timeout='1h';
unsafeWhen: statement_timeout is shorter than a long-running CREATE INDEX / backfill — the operation is killed mid-flight, and for a concurrent build leaves an INVALID index behind
lock_timeout is longer than statement_timeout, or lock_timeout is left at 0 (infinite) — the migration parks the whole table's lock queue
Application connections and migration connections share the same timeout profile — app connections want a short statement_timeout (~15s), migrations want a long one
safeRewrite: Give migration sessions their own role with short lock_timeout (queue safety) and long statement_timeout (completion safety); give application sessions a short statement_timeout (~15s) and a short lock_timeout (~10s). Never run a long backfill under an app-profile statement_timeout.
edgeCases: A concurrent index build killed by statement_timeout leaves an INVALID index that still incurs write overhead until dropped
statement_timeout does not fire while a statement is *waiting* for a lock — that window is governed by lock_timeout
versionNotes: statement_timeout since 7.3; lock_timeout since 9.3
_correction: statement_timeout was introduced in PostgreSQL 7.3 (2002), not 8.3. (lock_timeout since 9.3 is correct.)

title: A long-running or idle-in-transaction session prevents ACCESS EXCLUSIVE acquisition indefinitely
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (never granted while the blocker lives)
safeWhen: No session holds an open transaction that has touched (or could touch) the target table beyond a bounded time
idle_in_transaction_session_timeout is set so orphaned transactions are reaped
unsafeWhen: An idle-in-transaction session holds ACCESS SHARE from an earlier SELECT: the DDL's ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request waits forever (this is a legitimate wait, so the deadlock detector will NOT break it)
Without a lock_timeout, the parked request converts a single stuck client into a full-table outage per entry lock-queue-ae-behind-long-read
safeRewrite: Before DDL, query pg_stat_activity for state='idle in transaction' and long-running xacts (now() - xact_start), and use pg_blocking_pids() to find blockers; terminate or wait them out. Set idle_in_transaction_session_timeout on the app role, and always pair the DDL with a short lock_timeout so a stuck blocker aborts the DDL instead of the table.
edgeCases: Connection poolers (PgBouncer transaction mode misuse) and abandoned app transactions are the usual source of idle-in-transaction blockers
Prepared (two-phase) transactions hold their locks across sessions/restarts until COMMIT/ROLLBACK PREPARED — an orphaned prepared xact blocks DDL indefinitely and survives disconnect
pg_dump/pg_dump-based backups hold ACCESS SHARE for the whole run
versionNotes: idle_in_transaction_session_timeout since 9.6; pg_blocking_pids() since 9.6

title: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY completion is gated by the longest concurrent transaction
lockMode: SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (held for the entire multi-phase build)
sqlPattern: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY idx ON t (col);
safeWhen: No long-running transactions or old snapshots exist that could reference the table — CIC must wait for all such transactions to terminate before it can finish
The table has no leftover INVALID index from a prior failed attempt
You accept that the build does two full scans (dwell scales with row count) and holds SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE (blocks other DDL / concurrent builds, but not DML)
unsafeWhen: A single old snapshot anywhere in the cluster (idle-in-transaction, long analytics query, another CIC) forces CIC to wait after its second scan — the build hangs, keeping SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE and, critically, a low backend_xmin that stalls VACUUM cluster-wide (see long-txn-xmin-horizon-bloat-amplification)
Run inside an explicit transaction block — CIC is not allowed inside a transaction and will error
safeRewrite: Confirm no long transactions before starting (pg_stat_activity, and backend_xmin lag). Monitor progress with pg_stat_progress_create_index (12+). On failure, DROP INDEX CONCURRENTLY the leftover INVALID index (or REINDEX INDEX CONCURRENTLY) before retrying. Keep application transactions short during the build so the final wait phase does not stall.
edgeCases: Failure (deadlock, uniqueness violation, cancellation) leaves an INVALID index: it is ignored for queries but still incurs write/update overhead until dropped
Only one concurrent index build can run on a given table at a time (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE self-conflicts)
CIC itself acts as a long transaction: its snapshot holds back VACUUM's ability to remove dead tuples on ALL tables for the build's duration
REINDEX CONCURRENTLY leaves an extra invalid index with a _ccnew/_ccold suffix on failure
versionNotes: CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY since 8.2; REINDEX CONCURRENTLY since 12; pg_stat_progress_create_index since 12

PostgreSQL 14 narrowed the set of concurrent-index-build transactions CIC must wait on at the end (only when involved indexes are partial or have non-simple-column expressions), reducing cross-table stalls

title: A held xmin horizon blocks VACUUM and amplifies the cost/dwell of later DDL
lockMode: none (MVCC visibility horizon, not a table lock)
safeWhen: The oldest transaction/snapshot horizon (backend_xmin) advances promptly so VACUUM can reclaim dead tuples and the table stays compact
unsafeWhen: Any of: a long-running transaction, an idle-in-transaction session, an abandoned/consumerless replication slot (xmin never advances), a running CIC, or hot_standby_feedback=on with a long standby query holds the global xmin

VACUUM then cannot remove dead tuples, bloat grows, and a subsequent SCAN DDL (dwell scales with live+dead rows) or REWRITE DDL (dwell scales with bloated bytes, including TOAST) holds its ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock proportionally longer — the load-aware amplification of an otherwise 'fast' operation
safeRewrite: Before large DDL: VACUUM the table; verify the horizon is current via min(backend_xmin) in pg_stat_activity, pg_replication_slots.xmin, and pg_stat_replication.backend_xmin. Set idle_in_transaction_session_timeout, drop stale replication slots, and keep transactions short. Treat bloat as an input to whether a 'metadata-only'-looking rewrite is actually cheap.
edgeCases: A replication slot with no active consumer pins xmin forever and is the classic silent bloat cause
TOAST (out-of-line) storage bloats separately from the main heap; a REWRITE must rewrite TOAST too
backend_xmin held by a standby via hot_standby_feedback is invisible in the primary's pg_stat_activity — check pg_stat_replication
versionNotes: pg_stat_activity.backend_xmin since 9.4; pg_replication_slots since 9.4

title: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE DDL replayed on a hot standby cancels standby queries (or stalls replay)
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (replayed by WAL recovery on the standby)
safeWhen: The standby has no queries touching the affected table when the AE WAL record is replayed, OR they finish within max_standby_streaming_delay
The primary does not run DDL/LOCK TABLE/TRUNCATE/DROP during known heavy standby-read windows
unsafeWhen: The primary runs any DDL that takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (ALTER TABLE, DROP, TRUNCATE, explicit LOCK): the WAL AE-lock record conflicts with in-flight standby queries on that table. The primary never waits for standby queries, so recovery on the standby must choose

With bounded delay (default max_standby_streaming_delay = 30s, max_standby_archive_delay = 30s), the conflicting standby query is CANCELED after the delay (ERROR: canceling statement due to conflict with recovery, SQLSTATE 40001)
With delay = -1 (wait forever), recovery pauses instead — replica lag grows unbounded until the standby query ends
safeRewrite: Schedule ACCESS EXCLUSIVE DDL when the standby is idle, or drain long analytics off the standby during the migration window. Tune max_standby_streaming_delay to a bounded, non-negative value that trades tolerable lag against tolerable cancellation. Monitor pg_stat_replication replay lag around the DDL.
edgeCases: Replaying DROP DATABASE terminates the entire conflicting session on the standby, not just the running statement
The same DDL is thus doubly hazardous: it queues the primary (entry lock-queue-ae-behind-long-read) AND cancels standby readers
Cascading standbys propagate the conflict downstream
versionNotes: Hot standby since 9.0; hot_standby defaults to on since PostgreSQL 10; max_standby_streaming_delay / max_standby_archive_delay default to 30s

title: VACUUM cleanup WAL records cancel standby queries via early-cleanup conflict
lockMode: none (snapshot/buffer visibility conflict during WAL replay)
safeWhen: Standby queries are short relative to max_standby_streaming_delay, OR hot_standby_feedback=on holds the primary's cleanup horizon back
unsafeWhen: VACUUM/autovacuum on the primary removes row versions still visible to a standby snapshot ('early cleanup'): replaying that cleanup record conflicts and cancels the standby query after the delay

Even VACUUM that removed no rows can conflict: setting a page all-visible in the visibility map conflicts with standby queries (especially index-only scans) that cannot yet treat those rows as all-visible
A DDL/backfill that generates dead tuples on the primary triggers the autovacuum that later produces these cancelling cleanup records
safeRewrite: Set hot_standby_feedback=on so the primary does not clean up rows still needed by standby queries (accepting some primary bloat), or raise max_standby_streaming_delay, or keep standby queries short. For migration backfills, throttle to limit the dead-tuple burst that autovacuum will later replay.
edgeCases: Index-only scans on the standby are especially conflict-prone because they depend on the visibility map agreeing with their snapshot
hot_standby_feedback moves the cost to primary bloat (see hot-standby-feedback-bloat-feedback-loop)
versionNotes: Behavior documented for hot standby since 9.0; visibility-map all-visible conflicts apply to index-only scans (9.2+)

title: hot_standby_feedback trades standby cancellations for primary bloat — the amplification loop
lockMode: none (propagates the standby's xmin horizon to the primary)
safeWhen: Standby queries are bounded in duration, so the primary's cleanup horizon is only briefly held back and bloat stays negligible
You need long-running standby reads and can tolerate modest, monitored primary bloat
unsafeWhen: A long-running standby query with hot_standby_feedback=on pins the primary's xmin: primary VACUUM stalls, dead tuples accumulate, and any later DDL that scans or rewrites the bloated table holds its ACCESS EXCLUSIVE lock longer — closing the loop with long-txn-xmin-horizon-bloat-amplification
A standby that disconnects/reconnects can transiently release then re-pin the horizon, complicating VACUUM scheduling
safeRewrite: Bound standby query runtime (statement_timeout on the standby) so feedback cannot pin the primary horizon indefinitely; monitor pg_stat_replication.backend_xmin and primary dead-tuple counts (pg_stat_all_tables.n_dead_tup); decide per-workload between cancellation risk (feedback off) and bloat risk (feedback on) rather than defaulting blindly.
edgeCases: Note the cleanup situation with feedback on is no worse than running those same queries directly on the primary — the bloat is the price of offloading
Feedback propagates through cascading standbys (9.5+), so a leaf standby's long query can pin the top primary
versionNotes: hot_standby_feedback since 9.1; cascading propagation since 9.5

title: Table/index-rewriting DDL floods WAL and amplifies replica lag under load
lockMode: ACCESS EXCLUSIVE (rewriting ALTER) or SHARE (non-concurrent CREATE INDEX — blocks writes, not reads)
safeWhen: The operation avoids a heap rewrite entirely: e.g. ADD COLUMN with a constant (non-volatile) DEFAULT is metadata-only since PG 11 and emits no bulk WAL
Off-peak execution where the extra WAL volume will not saturate streaming replication bandwidth
unsafeWhen: A full-table rewrite (ALTER COLUMN TYPE, ADD COLUMN with volatile default, SET TABLESPACE, some SET LOGGED changes) or a non-concurrent CREATE INDEX generates WAL proportional to table+index (+TOAST) bytes

On a busy primary that WAL burst outpaces the replication link: standby write/flush/replay lag spikes, delaying every standby reader — and when the trailing ACCESS EXCLUSIVE commit is finally replayed it also cancels standby queries (replica-ae-replay-cancels-standby-queries)
safeRewrite: Avoid the rewrite: add a new nullable/defaulted column and backfill in throttled batches; use pg_repack or pgroll for type changes and reclaims; build indexes with CREATE INDEX CONCURRENTLY. Throttle backfills and run during low-traffic windows. Monitor pg_stat_replication write_lag/flush_lag/replay_lag during the operation.
edgeCases: Non-concurrent CREATE INDEX takes SHARE (blocks writes but allows reads) — different blocking profile from a rewriting ALTER's ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
A volatile DEFAULT (e.g. clock_timestamp(), random()) forces a rewrite even on PG 11+; only stable/immutable defaults get the metadata-only fast path
The WAL flood also lengthens the crash-recovery / failover window on the standby
versionNotes: ADD COLUMN with a non-volatile DEFAULT is metadata-only (no rewrite, constant time) since PostgreSQL 11; earlier versions rewrite the whole table under ACCESS EXCLUSIVE

title: Lock-order and lock-upgrade deadlocks around queued DDL
lockMode: escalating (e.g. ACCESS SHARE held → ACCESS EXCLUSIVE requested), or multi-table ACCESS EXCLUSIVE
safeWhen: The DDL transaction acquires all needed locks up front in a single, consistent order and does not upgrade a lock it already holds
Single-statement DDL transactions are used so no weaker lock is held before the ACCESS EXCLUSIVE request
unsafeWhen: A transaction already holding a weaker lock on the table (e.g. from an earlier SELECT) then requests ACCESS EXCLUSIVE while another session holds a conflicting lock — a lock upgrade that can deadlock

Multi-table DDL (e.g. adding a foreign key takes ACCESS EXCLUSIVE on BOTH tables) acquired in inconsistent order across concurrent migrations deadlocks; the detector cancels one victim (SQLSTATE 40P01)
safeRewrite: Acquire locks in a consistent, documented order across all migrations; take the strongest needed lock explicitly at transaction start (LOCK TABLE ... IN ACCESS EXCLUSIVE MODE) under a short lock_timeout rather than upgrading mid-transaction; keep DDL to single-statement transactions; split FK creation into ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT VALID (brief lock) then VALIDATE CONSTRAINT (SHARE UPDATE EXCLUSIVE, no long AE hold).
edgeCases: Postgres only breaks a cycle when it is a true deadlock; a one-directional indefinite wait (blocker never releases) is NOT broken and needs lock_timeout instead
Adding a foreign key locks both referencing and referenced tables — the referenced side is easy to forget as a blocking surface
versionNotes: ADD CONSTRAINT ... NOT VALID + VALIDATE CONSTRAINT split available since 9.1 (FK) / 9.2 (CHECK)
